# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Mammalian Cell Culture. List the essential components of a typical culture medium.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

Mammalian cell culture is the process of growing animal cells under controlled, sterile conditions in vitro (outside their original organism) within artificial nutrient media and vessels.

Essential components of a typical culture medium:
1.  **Basal salt solution** (e.g., Earle's salts) — provides osmotic balance and ions.
2.  **Amino acids** — 13 essential and 7 non-essential (e.g., L-glutamine 2 mM).
3.  **Glucose** — primary energy source (5.5–25 mM).
4.  **Vitamins** — B-complex co-factors.
5.  **Buffer system** — bicarbonate (NaHCO₃) for CO₂/HCO₃⁻ equilibrium.
6.  **pH indicator** — phenol red.
7.  **Serum (5–20% FBS)** — supplies growth factors, hormones, attachment proteins, and lipids.
8.  **Antibiotics** (optional) — penicillin–streptomycin for contamination control.

---

### Q2. Why is 5% CO₂ supplied in the incubator? What is the role of HEPES in the medium?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

The culture medium contains sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃) as its primary buffer. To maintain the physiological pH of 7.2–7.4, the HCO₃⁻/H₂CO₃ equilibrium must be balanced. At 37 °C, the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation shows that a **5% CO₂ atmosphere** (partial pressure ≈ 36 mmHg) is required to keep the bicarbonate buffer in the correct ratio. Without CO₂, the medium rapidly becomes alkaline.

**Role of HEPES:** HEPES (N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N'-2-ethanesulfonic acid) is a **zwitterionic organic buffer** added (10–25 mM) to maintain stable pH *outside* the CO₂ incubator — for example, during long microscope observations, transport, or manipulations on the bench. It has a pKa of 7.55 at 37 °C, making it ideal for the physiological pH range, and it is **CO₂-independent**, unlike bicarbonate.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **Sub-culturing and Cell Enumeration**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

(a) Describe the step-by-step procedure for sub-culturing an adherent mammalian cell line using the trypsinization method. (7 marks)
(b) A cell suspension is diluted 1:1 with trypan blue, and the average count of viable (unstained) cells in the four corner squares of a hemocytometer is 82. Calculate the viable cell concentration in cells/mL. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure for Sub-culturing an Adherent Mammalian Cell Line:**

1.  Pre-warm complete medium, PBS, and 0.25% Trypsin-EDTA to 37 °C in a water bath for 15–20 minutes.
2.  Observe the culture under an inverted microscope. Proceed only when cells are 80–90% confluent.
3.  Sanitize the laminar air flow (LAF) cabinet with 70% ethanol; collect all sterile materials inside.
4.  Aspirate the exhausted (spent) medium from the flask using a sterile pipette.
5.  Wash the monolayer with 5–10 mL of **PBS without Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺** to remove residual serum, which would otherwise inhibit trypsin.
6.  Add 1–2 mL of 0.25% Trypsin-EDTA, ensuring complete coverage of the monolayer.
7.  Incubate the flask at 37 °C for 2–5 minutes. Observe under microscope; cells should round up and detach.
8.  Add 4–8 mL of **complete medium (with 10% serum)** to neutralize trypsin via serum α1-antitrypsin.
9.  Pipette gently 5–10 times to break clumps and obtain a single-cell suspension.
10. Take a 20 µL aliquot for trypan blue viability counting.
11. Inoculate the appropriate volume of cell suspension into new flasks based on the desired split ratio (e.g., 1:4).
12. Label the new flasks with cell line, passage number, date, and initials. Loosen the cap and place in a 37 °C, 5% CO₂ incubator.

**(b) Cell Concentration Calculation:**

Given:
*   Dilution factor $D = 2$ (1:1 with trypan blue).
*   Average count per large square $N = 82$.
*   Depth of chamber $= 0.1$ mm; each large square area $= 1$ mm².

The volume of one large square:
$$
V = 1 \text{ mm} \times 1 \text{ mm} \times 0.1 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ mm}^3 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mL}
$$

Cell density formula:
$$
C = N \times D \times 10^4
$$

Substituting:
$$
C = 82 \times 2 \times 10^4
$$

$$
C = 1.64 \times 10^6 \text{ cells/mL}
$$

> **Answer:** The viable cell concentration is **1.64 × 10⁶ cells/mL**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **Cryopreservation and Contamination Control**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

(a) Explain the principle and procedure of cryopreservation of mammalian cells. Why is DMSO used as a cryoprotectant? Why is slow freezing combined with fast thawing recommended? (7 marks)
(b) Discuss the sources and detection methods of mycoplasma contamination in cell culture. How is aseptic technique enforced in a BSL-2 laboratory? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cryopreservation Principle and Procedure:**

Cryopreservation halts all biological activity of cells by storing them at ultra-low temperatures in **liquid nitrogen (–196 °C)**. At this temperature, molecular motion virtually ceases, allowing indefinite storage of cells without genetic or phenotypic drift.

**Procedure:**
1.  Harvest cells in **log phase** (60–80% confluence) via standard trypsinization.
2.  Centrifuge the suspension at ~200 × g for 5 minutes; discard the supernatant.
3.  Resuspend the cell pellet in **cold freezing medium: 90% FBS + 10% DMSO**, adjusting density to ~1 × 10⁶ cells/mL.
4.  Aliquot 1 mL per cryovial; label with cell line, passage, and date.
5.  Place vials in a **Mr. Frosty container (isopropanol-based)** to achieve a controlled cooling rate of **–1 °C/min**; transfer to a –80 °C freezer overnight.
6.  Next day, transfer vials to the **liquid nitrogen (LN₂) vapor phase** for long-term storage.
7.  **Thawing:** Rapidly thaw the vial in a **37 °C water bath for ~90 seconds**, dilute immediately in pre-warmed complete medium, centrifuge, and resuspend for inoculation.

**Why DMSO?** Dimethyl sulfoxide is a **penetrating cryoprotectant**. It crosses the plasma membrane, lowers the intracellular freezing point, and dehydrates the cell moderately by creating an osmotic gradient outward. This **prevents the formation of lethal intracellular ice crystals** that would rupture organelles and the cell membrane. The standard concentration is **10% v/v**; higher concentrations become cytotoxic.

**Why Slow Freezing, Fast Thawing?**
*   *Slow freezing* (–1 °C/min) allows water to move out of the cell osmotically, preventing intracellular ice nucleation.
*   *Fast thawing* (37 °C) prevents **recrystallization** — the conversion of small, harmless ice microcrystals back into large, lethal ice crystals — and minimizes the exposure time of the cells to hypertonic DMSO at high temperatures.

**(b) Mycoplasma Contamination and Aseptic Technique:**

Mycoplasmas (class *Mollicutes*) are tiny wall-less prokaryotes (~0.2–0.3 µm diameter) that are a **major hidden threat** in cell culture. They pass through 0.22 µm filters, do not cause turbidity, and grow slowly, making them undetectable by eye. Sources include contaminated FBS, operator respiratory droplets, oral/mycoplasma contamination of cell lines from other labs, and lapses in aseptic technique. Mycoplasmas **deplete essential nutrients** (especially arginine and glucose), produce toxic metabolites (NH₃, H₂O₂), and **alter gene expression and chromosomal structure**, rendering experimental data unreliable.

**Detection Methods:**
*   **PCR amplification of 16S rRNA** — the gold standard, sensitive to 1–10 organisms.
*   **DNA fluorochrome staining** (Hoechst 33258) — extranuclear punctate fluorescence on the cell surface.
*   **Broth–agar microbiological culture** — colonies appear in 3–14 days.
*   **Commercial ELISA / MycoAlert kits** — detect mycoplasma-specific enzymes.

**Aseptic Technique Enforcement in a BSL-2 Lab:**
1.  Work **only inside a Class II Type A2 LAF cabinet** with HEPA-filtered (0.22 µm) sterile air.
2.  Wear **sterile gloves, lab coat, mask, and hair net**; swab all bottle necks with 70% ethanol.
3.  Use **sterile, single-use disposable pipettes** and consumables; never use cracked or chipped glassware.
4.  **Sterilize reagents** by autoclaving (heat-stable) or 0.22 µm membrane filtration (heat-labile).
5.  Quarantine and **screen all new incoming cell lines** for mycoplasma before use.
6.  Maintain **dedicated media and reagents** for each cell line to avoid cross-contamination.
7.  **Decontaminate** spills immediately with 10% bleach or 70% ethanol.
8.  Discard contaminated cultures by **autoclaving**, never by sink disposal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> *   In (a), students often **omit the thawing step** — full marks require both freezing *and* thawing protocols.
> *   In (b), students often **confuse mycoplasma with bacteria or viruses** — remember, mycoplasma are **wall-less bacteria**, not viruses, and they are **resistant to penicillin/streptomycin** (which target cell walls and protein synthesis, respectively). A specific mention of this is what elevates a 7/7 answer to a perfect 14/14.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mammalian Cell Culture In Vitro**

*   **In vitro** means "in glass" — cells grown in artificial vessels, outside the organism.
*   Standard culture conditions: **37 °C, 5% CO₂, 95% humidity, pH 7.2–7.4, 280–320 mOsm/kg**.
*   **5% CO₂** maintains the HCO₃⁻/CO₂ buffer via the Henderson-Hasselbalch equilibrium.
*   **Primary cultures** have finite lifespan; **continuous cell lines** (e.g., HeLa, CHO) are immortalized and grow indefinitely.
*   **Adherent cells** (HeLa, Vero) need TC-treated plastic; **suspension cells** (hybridomas, Jurkat) do not.
*   **DMEM, RPMI-1640, MEM** are the three most common basal media.
*   **L-Glutamine** degrades to ammonia — use **Glutamax** (stable dipeptide) for sensitive cultures.
*   **Aseptic technique** is the #1 priority — work only inside a **LAF (Class II) cabinet**; swab with 70% ethanol.
*   **Autoclave** heat-stable items at 121 °C / 15 psi / 15 min; **filter (0.22 µm)** heat-labile media/serum.
*   **EDTA chelates Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺**; **trypsin** cleaves adhesion proteins at **Lys-Arg** bonds.
*   **Neutralize trypsin** with serum-containing medium (α1-antitrypsin).
*   **Sub-culture at 80–90% confluence**; calculate split ratio based on surface area.
*   **Hemocytometer formula:** $C = N \times D \times 10^4$ cells/mL.
*   **Trypan blue exclusion** — dead cells with leaky membranes turn blue; viable cells exclude the dye.
*   **Cryopreservation** uses **10% DMSO** in FBS; controlled cooling at **–1 °C/min**; storage at **–196 °C in LN₂**.
*   **Slow freeze, fast thaw** — prevents intracellular ice crystal formation and recrystallization.
*   **Mycoplasma** are wall-less bacteria (~0.3 µm) — they pass through 0.22 µm filters and are invisible in culture.
*   **Mycoplasma detection:** PCR (16S rRNA), Hoechst DNA staining, or specialized agar culture.
*   **Hayflick limit** of ~50 ± 10 population doublings for normal diploid cell strains.
*   **Population Doubling Time (Td):** 18–24 h for most cell lines; formula $T_d = t \cdot \ln(2) / \ln(N_t/N_0)$.
*   **FBS** (5–20%) provides growth factors, attachment factors, lipids, and protease inhibitors.
*   **Penicillin–streptomycin** is *ineffective* against mycoplasma (no cell wall to target).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disaggregation of Tissue, Primary Culture Establishment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT411 - PLANT AND ANIMAL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Disaggregation of Tissue, Primary Culture Establishment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Disaggregation of Tissue?

**Disaggregation** is the mechanical or enzymatic process by which intact animal tissue is broken down into a suspension of individual cells, cell clusters, or small tissue fragments suitable for *in vitro* culture. It is the foundational step that bridges whole-organ biology with cell culture technology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Disaggregation is the dissociation of cells from the parent tissue using physical, chemical, or enzymatic means to obtain a viable single-cell suspension or explant suitable for initiating a primary culture.

## 1.2 What is Primary Culture Establishment?

A **primary culture** is the first culture established from a freshly disaggregated tissue or organ explant, prior to any sub-culturing (passaging). It represents the *in vitro* survival and proliferation of cells taken directly from the organism, and its success is wholly dependent on the disaggregation method chosen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Primary cultures have a finite lifespan and are *not* immortalized. They must be distinguished from continuous (established) cell lines, which are capable of indefinite proliferation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **brick wall (tissue)** held together by:
- **Mortar** (extracellular matrix / collagen / fibronectin)
- **Velcro patches** between adjacent bricks (cell–cell adhesion molecules like cadherins, integrins)

**Disaggregation** is like dismantling this wall:
- **Mechanical disaggregation** = physically chiseling bricks apart (fast, but many bricks break)
- **Enzymatic disaggregation** = dissolving the mortar and undoing the Velcro (slower, but the bricks remain intact and functional)

Once you have a pile of intact bricks (cells), you spread them on a fresh mortar bed (the culture vessel with growth medium) — that spreading is **primary culture establishment**. The cells then re-attach and grow, like bricks being relaid into a new wall.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard incubation temperature** for mammalian cell culture: **37 °C**
- **Optimal pH of culture medium**: **7.2 – 7.4**
- **Osmolality of physiological media**: **280 – 320 mOsm/kg**
- **CO₂ tension for bicarbonate-buffered systems**: **5 % CO₂**
- **Typical cell yield from rodent tissue**: 10⁶ – 10⁷ cells per gram of tissue
- **Acceptable viability post-disaggregation**: **≥ 90 %** (assessed by Trypan Blue exclusion)

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test the standard cell-culture parameters (37 °C, 5 % CO₂, pH 7.4). Memorize these as a **fingerprint** of animal cell technology.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Principles Underlying Disaggregation

Tissue integrity in animals is maintained by:
- **Cell–Cell junctions** (desmosomes, tight junctions, adherens junctions, gap junctions)
- **Cell–Extracellular Matrix (ECM) attachments** (integrins binding fibronectin, laminin, collagen)
- **Extracellular Matrix scaffold** (collagen, elastin, proteoglycans)

Therefore, any disaggregation technique must selectively disrupt these adhesive structures while preserving **membrane integrity, receptor function, and metabolic viability**.

## 2.2 Classification of Disaggregation Techniques

Disaggregation methods are broadly classified into three families:

### A) Mechanical Disaggregation
- **Chopping / Mincing** with scalpels or scissors
- **Sieving / Pressing** through a metal mesh or sieve
- **Syringing** through a fine-bore needle (e.g., 19G → 23G sequentially)
- **Scraping** with a rubber policeman (for soft tissues like epithelium)
- **Pipetting** repeatedly with serological pipettes
- **Magnet stirring** with a Teflon-coated stir bar

### B) Enzymatic Disaggregation
- **Trypsin** — cleaves peptide bonds at the C-terminal side of lysine/arginine; digests adhesion proteins
- **Collagenase** — cleaves the helical region of native collagen; preferred for connective tissue
- **Hyaluronidase** — degrades hyaluronic acid in ECM
- **Elastase** — digests elastin
- **Chymotrypsin** — similar to trypsin but with different specificity
- **Dispase** — neutral protease; gentle; preserves epithelial sheets
- **DNase I** — co-administered to digest DNA released from lysed cells (prevents clumping)

### C) Chemical Disaggregation
- **Chelating agents** — **EDTA (Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid)** or **EGTA** sequester Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions, disrupting cadherin-mediated adhesion. Used at **0.02 % (w/v)** for 5–10 minutes at 37 °C.

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" of Each Step

| Step | Why It Is Done | How It Works |
|------|----------------|--------------|
| Tissue excision | Obtain fresh, sterile sample | Rapid dissection under aseptic conditions |
| Washing | Remove blood, debris, microbes | HBSS or PBS with antibiotics |
| Mincing | Increase surface area for enzyme access | Scalpels + Petri dish, fragments ~1–3 mm |
| Enzymatic digestion | Disrupt ECM and cell–cell junctions | Trypsin 0.25 % or collagenase 200 U/mL, 30–60 min, 37 °C |
| Mechanical agitation | Aid dissociation of partially digested tissue | Pipetting or magnetic stirring |
| Filtration | Remove undigested debris and tissue clumps | Cell strainer (100 µm or 70 µm nylon mesh) |
| Centrifugation | Pellet cells and remove enzyme | 80–200 × g, 5–10 min |
| Resuspension | Place cells in growth medium | Complete medium (e.g., DMEM + 10 % FBS) |
| Counting & viability | Standardize seeding density | Hemocytometer + Trypan Blue exclusion |
| Inoculation | Establish the primary culture | Seed at 10⁵ – 10⁶ cells/mL into culture vessel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Trypsinization** is a KTU-favorite modification: tissues are exposed to trypsin at **4 °C for 6–24 h** followed by warm incubation. This prevents over-digestion and improves viability for sensitive primary cultures.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Value / Method | Notes |
|-----------|---------------|-------|
| Trypsin concentration | 0.25 % (w/v) in PBS | Often with 0.02 % EDTA |
| Collagenase concentration | 200 – 2000 U/mL | Type I or Type IV |
| Trypsinization temperature | 37 °C (warm) or 4 °C (cold) | Cold = gentler |
| Incubation time | 10 – 60 min | Monitor under microscope |
| Inactivation of trypsin | FBS in medium (10 %) | Or add soybean trypsin inhibitor |
| Centrifugation speed | 80 – 200 × g | Brake off for fragile cells |
| Cell strainer mesh | 70 µm or 100 µm | Removes clumps |
| Seeding density | 1 × 10⁵ – 1 × 10⁶ cells/mL | Depends on cell type |
| Viability threshold | ≥ 90 % | Trypan Blue exclusion |
| Tissue fragment size (mincing) | ~1 – 3 mm³ | Maximizes enzyme penetration |

## 2.5 Engineering / Industrial Utility

- **Vaccine production** (e.g., Vero cells for polio and rabies vaccines)
- **Monoclonal antibody generation** (hybridoma technology requires splenocyte primary cultures)
- **Cytotoxicity testing** and **drug discovery** (primary hepatocytes, neurons)
- **Regenerative medicine** (chondrocytes, keratinocytes, stem cells)
- **Tissue engineering** and **3D organoid construction**

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of disaggregation method directly determines whether the resulting primary culture is **epithelial-rich, fibroblast-rich, or mixed**, which is critical for downstream applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Protocols, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Quantitative Framework: Cell Yield and Viability

Before any culture, the biotechnologist must know two numbers:
- **Total viable cell count** (used to calculate seeding density)
- **Viability percentage** (used as a QC check)

### 3.1.1 Cell Count Using a Hemocytometer

The standard hemocytometer chamber has:
- A depth of **0.1 mm** (1 × 10⁻⁴ m)
- A counting grid of **1 mm²** (1 × 10⁻⁶ m²)
- Volume over one large central square = **0.1 mm³ = 1 × 10⁻⁴ mL**

**Formula (no absolute value bars that break markdown — use \mid):**

$$
\text{Cells per mL} = \frac{\text{Average count per large square}}{1} \times \text{Dilution factor} \times 10^{4}
$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{square}} &= 1 \,\text{mm}^2 \times 0.1 \,\text{mm} = 0.1 \,\text{mm}^3 \\
&= 0.1 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mL} \quad (\text{since } 1 \,\text{mL} = 1\,\text{cm}^3 = 10^3 \,\text{mm}^3) \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{mL} \\
\therefore \text{Cells per mL} &= \frac{\text{Count}}{1 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{mL}} \times \text{Dilution} \\
&= \text{Count} \times \text{Dilution} \times 10^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Viability by Trypan Blue Exclusion

Trypan Blue is a membrane-impermeant dye. **Live cells** (intact membrane) **exclude** the dye; **dead cells** (compromised membrane) **take it up**.

$$
\% \text{Viability} = \frac{\text{Number of unstained (viable) cells}}{\text{Total number of cells counted}} \times 100
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A researcher disaggregates 1.5 g of murine liver tissue. The cell suspension is resuspended in 10 mL of medium. A 100 µL aliquot is mixed with 100 µL of 0.4 % Trypan Blue (1:1 dilution). Using a hemocytometer, the four corner squares of the central grid give counts (unstained / stained) of: 78/4, 82/6, 85/3, 75/5. Calculate:
(a) Total viable cells
(b) Viability percentage
(c) Cells per gram of tissue
(d) Whether the preparation is acceptable for primary culture

### Solution

**Step 1 — Average cell count per large square:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average unstained} &= \frac{78 + 82 + 85 + 75}{4} = \frac{320}{4} = 80 \\
\text{Average stained} &= \frac{4 + 6 + 3 + 5}{4} = \frac{18}{4} = 4.5 \\
\text{Average total} &= 80 + 4.5 = 84.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cells per mL of diluted suspension:**

$$
\text{Cells/mL (diluted)} = 84.5 \times 10^{4} = 8.45 \times 10^{5} \,\text{cells/mL}
$$

**Step 3 — Correct for the 1:1 Trypan Blue dilution factor = 2:**

$$
\text{Cells/mL (original)} = 8.45 \times 10^{5} \times 2 = 1.69 \times 10^{6} \,\text{cells/mL}
$$

**Step 4 — Total viable cells in 10 mL suspension:**

$$
\text{Total viable cells} = 1.69 \times 10^{6} \times 10 = 1.69 \times 10^{7} \,\text{cells}
$$

**Step 5 — Viability percentage:**

$$
\% \text{ Viability} = \frac{80}{84.5} \times 100 = 94.67 \,\% \approx 94.7 \,\%
$$

**Step 6 — Cells per gram of tissue:**

$$
\text{Yield} = \frac{1.69 \times 10^{7} \,\text{cells}}{1.5 \,\text{g}} = 1.13 \times 10^{7} \,\text{cells/g}
$$

**Step 7 — QC Verdict:** Viability **94.7 %** ≥ 90 % threshold → **ACCEPTABLE** for primary culture initiation.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "T" for "Trouble" — if **T**rypan Blue enters, the cell is in **T**rouble (dead).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Disaggregation Methods

| Feature | Mechanical (Mincing + Sieving) | Warm Trypsin | Cold Trypsin | Collagenase | EDTA Chelation |
|---------|-------------------------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| Cell yield | Moderate | High | High | Very High | Low–Moderate |
| Viability | High | Moderate | Very High | Very High | High |
| Speed | Fast | Moderate | Slow (overnight) | Slow (1–2 h) | Fast |
| Surface protein damage | None | Yes (strips receptors) | Mild | Minimal | Mild |
| ECM disruption | Partial | Moderate | Moderate | Excellent | None |
| Best for | Soft tissue, biopsy | Embryonic, bulk | Sensitive primary | Connective tissue, liver | Epithelial monolayers |
| Risk of over-digestion | Low | High | Low | Low | None |
| Common in KTU questions | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ |

## 3.4 Primary Culture Establishment — Stepwise Protocol

1. **Tissue procurement** — animal sacrificed humanely; target organ excised aseptically
2. **Washing** — 3× washes in HBSS/PBS with antibiotics (Pen-Strep, Amphotericin B)
3. **Mincing** — fragments reduced to ~1–3 mm³ with crossed scalpels
4. **Enzymatic treatment** — 0.25 % Trypsin + 0.02 % EDTA, 37 °C, 20–30 min, OR 200 U/mL collagenase, 45–60 min
5. **Mechanical aid** — gentle pipetting every 10 min
6. **Filtration** — 100 µm cell strainer
7. **Centrifugation** — 100 × g, 5 min, discard supernatant
8. **Resuspension** — in complete growth medium (e.g., DMEM + 10 % FBS)
9. **Cell counting & viability** — hemocytometer + Trypan Blue
10. **Seeding** — 1 × 10⁵ – 1 × 10⁶ cells/mL in 25 cm² flask (T-flask)
11. **Incubation** — 37 °C, 5 % CO₂, humidified
12. **Monitoring** — daily observation; medium change every 2–3 days
13. **Subculture** (passage) — when monolayer reaches 80–90 % confluence (this converts the primary culture into a **secondary culture**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A primary culture becomes a **secondary culture** the *moment* it is sub-cultured (passaged). This distinction is a high-frequency question topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Workflow: From Tissue to Primary Culture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Tissue Excision] --> B[Wash in HBSS with Antibiotics]
    B --> C[Mince into 1 to 3 mm fragments]
    C --> D{Choose Disaggregation Method}
    D --> E1[Mechanical: Sieve or Syringe]
    D --> E2[Enzymatic: Trypsin 0.25 percent]
    D --> E3[Enzymatic: Collagenase 200 U per mL]
    D --> E4[Chemical: EDTA 0.02 percent]
    E1 --> F[Centrifuge 100 x g, 5 min]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Resuspend in Complete Medium]
    G --> H[Count and Viability Check]
    H --> I{Quality Control: Viability >= 90 percent}
    I -->|Yes| J[Seed into T-flask at 1E5 to 1E6 cells per mL]
    I -->|No| K[Discard or Repeat]
    J --> L[Incubate at 37 C, 5 percent CO2]
    L --> M[Monitor Daily, Change Medium every 2 to 3 days]
    M --> N[Primary Culture Established]
    N --> O[Subculture at 80 to 90 percent Confluence]
    O --> P[Secondary Culture]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mechanistic Overview of Cell Release

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IntactTissue[INTACT TISSUE]
        ECM1[Extracellular Matrix: Collagen Fibronectin]
        Jun1[Cell Cell Junctions: Cadherins Desmosomes]
    end
    subgraph Agents[DISAGGREGATION AGENTS]
        Ag1[Trypsin: Cleaves Adhesion Proteins]
        Ag2[Collagenase: Digests Collagen Helix]
        Ag3[EDTA: Chelates Ca2+ and Mg2+]
        Ag4[Mechanical Force: Shear Stress]
    end
    subgraph Output[RELEASED CELLS]
        Out1[Single Cells]
        Out2[Small Clusters]
        Out3[Cell Suspension]
    end
    ECM1 --> Ag2
    Jun1 --> Ag1
    Jun1 --> Ag3
    IntactTissue --> Ag4
    Ag1 --> Out1
    Ag2 --> Out1
    Ag3 --> Out1
    Ag4 --> Out2
    Out1 --> Out3
    Out2 --> Out3
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Disaggregation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Tissue Sample Received] --> Q1{Type of Tissue?}
    Q1 -->|Soft / Embryonic| P1[Trypsin 0.25 percent warm]
    Q1 -->|Dense Connective Tissue| P2[Collagenase 200 U per mL]
    Q1 -->|Epithelial Sheet| P3[Dispase or EDTA]
    Q1 -->|Loose / Fragile| P4[Cold Trypsin or Mechanical]
    P1 --> Q2{Application?}
    P2 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q2
    P4 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Cell line generation| R1[Single cell suspension required]
    Q2 -->|Organoid / 3D culture| R2[Small clusters preferred]
    Q2 -->|Histology / explant| R3[Mincing only, no enzyme]
    R1 --> End[Proceed to Plating]
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
```

## 4.4 Viability Assessment Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Cell Suspension + Trypan Blue 1:1] --> M[Load Hemocytometer]
    M --> C[Count under Phase Contrast Microscope]
    C --> V{Viable Cell?}
    V -->|Bright / Refractile| Live[Live Cell: Unstained]
    V -->|Dark Blue| Dead[Dead Cell: Stained]
    Live --> Sum[Sum and Calculate]
    Dead --> Sum
    Sum --> Formula[Percent Viability = Live / Total x 100]
    Formula --> QC{QC: Viability >= 90 percent}
    QC -->|Pass| Accept[Accept: Proceed to Plating]
    QC -->|Fail| Reject[Reject or Repeat Disaggregation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks)

### Question 1 (3 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define primary culture. What is the role of disaggregation in establishing a primary culture?**

**Model Answer:**
- A **primary culture** is a cell culture initiated directly from a freshly excised tissue, organ, or explant, without any prior sub-culturing. (1 mark)
- It represents the *in vitro* survival of cells taken directly from the donor organism and has a **finite lifespan**. (1 mark)
- **Disaggregation** is the prerequisite step that breaks down the three-dimensional tissue architecture into a **suspension of individual cells or small clusters** capable of attaching to a culture surface and proliferating. (1 mark)

### Question 2 (3 marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Why is EDTA used along with trypsin during enzymatic disaggregation?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Trypsin** cleaves peptide bonds of adhesion proteins but requires **divalent cations (Ca²⁺ / Mg²⁺)** for many cell–cell junctions to function. (1 mark)
- **EDTA** is a calcium/magnesium **chelating agent** that sequesters these ions, **loosening cell–cell junctions** and enhancing trypsin access. (1 mark)
- Together, EDTA + trypsin produce a **faster, more complete disaggregation** with reduced trypsin concentration and exposure time, improving viability. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **mechanical methods of tissue disaggregation**. Add a note on their advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **enzymatic method of disaggregation using trypsin**, including cold and warm trypsinization procedures.

#### Model Solution for (a) — Mechanical Disaggregation

**Definition (1 mark):** Mechanical disaggregation uses **physical force** to dissociate cells from tissues without chemical/enzymatic intervention.

**Methods (4 marks):**
1. **Chopping / Mincing** — tissue reduced to 1–3 mm³ fragments with crossed scalpels in a Petri dish in a small volume of medium
2. **Sieving / Pressing** — fragments forced through a **stainless steel or nylon mesh** (e.g., 100 µm pore size) using the flat end of a syringe plunger
3. **Syringing** — fragments aspirated and expelled repeatedly through a **wide-bore needle (19G → 23G)** to mechanically shear cells apart
4. **Pipetting** — repeated aspiration and expulsion using a serological pipette
5. **Magnetic stirring** — fragments placed in a flask with a stirrer bar; gentle stirring (50–100 rpm) for 30–60 min

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- No enzyme-related damage to surface receptors
- Quick, low-cost, no need for special reagents
- High viability when done gently

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Lower cell yield
- Significant mechanical damage and cell rupture
- Not suitable for dense or fibrous tissues

#### Model Solution for (b) — Enzymatic Disaggregation using Trypsin

**Principle (2 marks):**
- Trypsin is a **pancreatic serine protease** that cleaves peptide bonds on the **C-terminal side of lysine and arginine residues**
- It hydrolyses cell-surface adhesion proteins (e.g., cadherins, integrins) and ECM components
- The standard working concentration is **0.25 % (w/v) trypsin in Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺-free PBS**, often combined with **0.02 % EDTA**

**Warm Trypsinization (2 marks):**
- Tissue fragments incubated in trypsin solution at **37 °C for 20–30 min**
- Gentle agitation (pipetting) every 5–10 min
- Centrifuged at 100 × g, 5 min; pellet resuspended in **complete medium containing 10 % FBS**, which **inactivates residual trypsin** (soybean trypsin inhibitor can also be used)
- Yields high cell numbers in a short time

**Cold Trypsinization (2 marks):**
- Tissue is exposed to **trypsin at 4 °C for 6–24 hours**
- Trypsin penetrates slowly without digesting surface proteins
- Warmed to 37 °C for 20–30 min, after which the tissue readily dissociates
- **Advantages:** superior viability; better preservation of membrane receptors; ideal for sensitive primary cultures such as hepatocytes and neurons

**Disadvantage (1 mark):** Warm trypsin can strip surface antigens/receptors and cause cell clumping; over-digestion is the most common error.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the various **enzymes used for tissue disaggregation** and their specificities.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **step-by-step protocol for primary culture establishment** from animal tissue.

#### Model Solution for (a) — Enzymes for Disaggregation

| Enzyme | Specificity | Concentration | Best Used For |
|--------|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Trypsin** | Peptide bonds at Lys, Arg | 0.1–0.25 % | General purpose; soft tissue |
| **Collagenase** | Native collagen triple helix | 200–2000 U/mL | Connective tissue, liver, lung |
| **Dispase** | Neutral protease; gentle | 0.6 U/mL | Epithelial sheets, skin |
| **Hyaluronidase** | Hyaluronic acid | 0.01 % | Embryonic tissue, Wharton jelly |
| **Elastase** | Elastin | 0.25 % | Lung, vascular tissue |
| **Chymotrypsin** | Aromatic amino acids | 0.25 % | Supplementary to trypsin |
| **DNase I** | Free DNA (released from dead cells) | 0.1 mg/mL | Co-enzyme; reduces clumping |
| **Pronase** | Broad spectrum protease | 0.1 % | Difficult-to-disaggregate tissue |

**Key Points (3 marks):**
- Enzyme choice depends on **tissue origin and downstream application**
- **Collagenase is preferred for fibrous tissues** because trypsin cannot efficiently digest collagen
- **Dispase** is gentle and preserves **epithelial sheet integrity**, ideal for keratinocyte culture
- **DNase I is always used as a co-enzyme** to prevent reaggregation of cells by sticky DNA released from damaged cells

#### Model Solution for (b) — Protocol for Primary Culture

**Step 1 — Tissue procurement (1 mark):** Animal is humanely sacrificed; target organ excised aseptically using sterile instruments; placed in HBSS or transport medium with antibiotics (Penicillin 100 U/mL, Streptomycin 100 µg/mL, Amphotericin B 2.5 µg/mL).

**Step 2 — Washing (1 mark):** Tissue washed 3× in HBSS to remove blood, RBCs, and microbial contamination.

**Step 3 — Mincing (1 mark):** Tissue chopped into **1–3 mm³ fragments** using crossed scalpels; this maximizes surface area for enzyme access.

**Step 4 — Enzymatic digestion (1 mark):** Fragments incubated in **0.25 % trypsin + 0.02 % EDTA in PBS, 37 °C, 30 min**, with gentle pipetting every 10 min.

**Step 5 — Filtration (1 mark):** Suspension filtered through a **100 µm cell strainer** to remove clumps and undigested debris.

**Step 6 — Centrifugation & resuspension (1 mark):** Cells centrifuged at **100 × g for 5 min**, supernatant discarded, pellet resuspended in **complete growth medium (DMEM + 10 % FBS)**.

**Step 7 — Counting & viability (1 mark):** Cell count determined by hemocytometer; viability assessed by **Trypan Blue exclusion (must be ≥ 90 %)**.

**Step 8 — Seeding & incubation (1 mark):** Cells seeded at **1 × 10⁵ – 1 × 10⁶ cells/mL** into a **T25 flask** and incubated at **37 °C, 5 % CO₂, humidified atmosphere**. The primary culture is established upon attachment and outgrowth of cells.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 valuation pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing primary culture with cell line** — A primary culture becomes a **secondary culture the moment it is sub-cultured**. Cell lines are immortalized populations derived after many passages. Examiners deduct 1 mark per error of this kind.
> 2. **Forgetting 5 % CO₂** — Animal cell culture needs **bicarbonate-buffered medium + 5 % CO₂ atmosphere** to maintain pH 7.4. Stating only 37 °C is incomplete.
> 3. **Using "sterile" without specifying antibiotics in transport** — Always mention **Penicillin-Streptomycin + Amphotericin B** in HBSS for tissue transport.
> 4. **Reversing dilution factor in hemocytometer calculation** — A 1:1 dilution with Trypan Blue means **multiply by 2**, not divide.
> 5. **Confusing "warm" and "cold" trypsinization** — Cold trypsinization is **overnight at 4 °C**, *not* 37 °C. Mixing these up costs the entire sub-question.
> 6. **Missing cell strainer step** — Without filtration, clumps block the culture surface and produce uneven growth. Examiners consider this a "process gap".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 5.4.1 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Disaggregation** = breaking tissue into cells/clusters; **Primary culture** = first culture from such cells
- [ ] Three methods: **Mechanical, Enzymatic, Chemical (EDTA chelation)**
- [ ] **Trypsin** = 0.25 %, serine protease, cleaves at Lys/Arg
- [ ] **Collagenase** = best for **fibrous / connective tissue**
- [ ] **Dispase** = **gentle**, preserves epithelial sheets
- [ ] **EDTA** = chelates Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺; used **with** trypsin
- [ ] **DNase I** = co-enzyme; degrades sticky DNA from lysed cells
- [ ] **Warm trypsinization** = 37 °C, 20–30 min
- [ ] **Cold trypsinization** = 4 °C, 6–24 h → higher viability
- [ ] **Trypan Blue exclusion** = viability stain; live = unstained; dead = blue
- [ ] **Viability threshold** = **≥ 90 %** acceptable
- [ ] **Cell count formula** = average × dilution × 10⁴ cells/mL
- [ ] **Hemocytometer depth** = **0.1 mm**; volume over large square = **1 × 10⁻⁴ mL**
- [ ] **Centrifugation** = 80–200 × g, 5 min
- [ ] **Seeding density** = 1 × 10⁵ – 1 × 10⁶ cells/mL
- [ ] **Incubation** = 37 °C, 5 % CO₂, humidified
- [ ] **Primary → Secondary** transition occurs at the **first subculture / passage**
- [ ] **FBS (10 %)** inactivates residual trypsin
- [ ] **Cell strainer** = 70 µm or 100 µm nylon mesh

### 5.4.2 KTU High-Yield Mnemonics

- **"TED"** = **T**rypsin, **E**DTA, **D**Nase I — the classical enzyme cocktail
- **"Trypan Blue = Trouble"** — if blue enters, the cell is dead
- **"COLD = 4 °C over-Night, DELIberately"** — for cold trypsinization
- **"Primary = Pre-passage"** — no subculture has occurred
- **"5-37-7.4"** — 5 % CO₂, 37 °C, pH 7.4 = animal cell culture signature

### 5.4.3 Expected KTU Question Patterns

- **Define** primary culture, disaggregation, explant, viability, passage
- **Differentiate** mechanical vs enzymatic disaggregation
- **Explain** role of trypsin + EDTA
- **Describe** protocol for primary culture establishment
- **Calculate** cell number and viability from hemocytometer data
- **Compare** warm and cold trypsinization
- **Justify** enzyme selection for a given tissue type

> [!TIP]
> For numerical problems, always **state the dilution factor explicitly**, then apply the formula `Count × Dilution × 10⁴`. Always **end with a QC verdict** — this is the step examiners look for to award full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance of Cell Culture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT411 - PLANT AND ANIMAL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maintenance of Cell Culture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cell Culture Maintenance** refers to the systematic set of aseptic bioprocess operations required to preserve the viability, phenotypic stability, proliferative capacity, and genetic integrity of *in vitro* cultured cells over extended periods. Under the KTU 2024 scheme (Outcome-Based Education framework), it encompasses the periodic replenishment of spent medium, subculturing (passaging) of confluent monolayers or suspension cultures, monitoring of cell health parameters, detection and elimination of contamination, cryopreservation for long-term banking, and the rigorous documentation of cell line provenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Maintenance is NOT a single technique — it is an integrated **Quality Management System (QMS)** for living biological material. The examiner expects students to treat it as a continuous, cyclic protocol rather than a one-time procedure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (must be memorized verbatim for 2-mark definitions):**
> - **Passage Number (PDL - Population Doubling Level):** The number of times the cell population has doubled since primary culture initiation.
> - **Confluence:** The percentage of the growth surface covered by adherent cells (typically passaged at 80–95%).
> - **Split Ratio:** The dilution factor applied during subculture (e.g., 1:3, 1:5, 1:10).
> - **Cryopreservation:** Ultra-low temperature storage (typically in **Liquid Nitrogen at -196°C**) to halt all biological and biochemical activity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cell Hotel" Intuition

Imagine you are running a luxury long-stay hotel for very demanding, microscopic guests:

- **The Guests (Cells):** They continuously eat, drink, produce waste, and multiply.
- **The Room (Culture Flask):** Has a fixed size. When it gets crowded, the guests become stressed, fight for space, and stop multiplying.
- **The Housekeeping (You/Lab Technician):** Must (a) **refill the minibar** = change the spent medium every 2–3 days, (b) **move guests to bigger rooms** = subculture/passaging when confluent, (c) **check for pests** = mycoplasma/bacterial/fungal contamination screening, and (d) **put some guests into deep freeze** = cryopreserve a master stock so the hotel never runs out of its original VIPs.

If the housekeeping fails, the guests die, mutate, or get contaminated — ruining all downstream experiments. **Cell culture maintenance is exactly this housekeeping discipline, performed under sterile (germ-free) conditions.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Incubator Temperature (Mammalian) | **37°C ± 0.5°C** |
| Incubator CO₂ Tension | **5% ± 0.2% CO₂** |
| Incubator Relative Humidity | **~95%** |
| Cryopreservation Storage | **-196°C (Liquid Nitrogen vapor phase)** |
| Slow-Freezing Rate | **-1°C per minute** |
| Cell Viability Threshold for Use | **≥ 90% (by Trypan Blue exclusion)** |
| Typical Subculture Confluence | **80–95%** |
| Mycoplasma Screening Frequency | **Every 1–3 months** |
| Recommended Split Ratio Range | **1:2 to 1:20** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Cell Culture Maintenance

The maintenance workflow can be logically deconstructed into **five sequential operational pillars**. KTU examiners frequently frame 14-mark questions around these.

#### **Pillar 1 — Medium Renewal (Feeding)**

Cells metabolize glucose, glutamine, and amino acids while secreting lactate and ammonia. Once **lactate exceeds ~2–3 mM** and **ammonia exceeds ~0.5–1 mM**, the medium becomes cytotoxic.

- **Frequency:** Every 2–3 days for fast-growing lines (e.g., HeLa, HEK293).
- **Volume replaced:** 70–100% for adherent cultures; 50–80% for suspension.
- **Pre-warming:** Medium must be brought to **37°C** in a water bath to avoid temperature shock.

**Why?** Temperature shock causes cytoskeletal rearrangement, membrane phase-transition (lipid bilayer shifts from fluid to gel state below ~15°C), and delays the next division cycle.

#### **Pillar 2 — Subculturing / Passaging (The "Split")**

When adherent cells reach **80–95% confluence**, they exhibit **contact inhibition** and begin to differentiate or undergo apoptosis. The cell-to-cell signaling via gap junctions and cadherins triggers cell cycle arrest at the **G1/S checkpoint**.

**Why subculture?**
1. Prevent nutrient depletion.
2. Remove contact-inhibited, non-proliferating cells.
3. Prevent spontaneous transformation in long-passage cultures.
4. Expand cell numbers for downstream assays.

**The Split Ratio Decision (Hayflick-style logic):**
- Slow-growing primary cultures → 1:2 split.
- Robust continuous lines (HeLa, CHO) → 1:5 to 1:20.
- **Rule of thumb:** A 1:N split theoretically yields 3.32 × log₁₀(N) population doublings per passage.

#### **Pillar 3 — Contamination Monitoring**

Contamination is the **#1 cause of cell culture failure** in academic labs (estimated >20% loss rate globally).

| Contaminant | Detection Method | Time to Detect |
|---|---|---|
| Bacteria | Turbidity, Gram stain, smell | 1–3 days |
| Yeast/Fungi | Microscopy, turbidity | 2–7 days |
| Mycoplasma | PCR, Hoechst 33258 stain, ELISA | 1–7 days |
| Cross-contamination (other cell lines) | STR profiling, isoenzyme analysis | 1–2 weeks |
| Viral | Electron microscopy, cytopathic effects | Variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Antibiotics are NOT a substitute for aseptic technique.** Continuous antibiotic use masks low-level contamination, selects for resistant strains, and alters cell gene expression. KTU examiners reward students who advocate antibiotic-free media.

#### **Pillar 4 — Cryopreservation (Long-term Banking)**

Cryopreservation halts the biological clock. The challenge is that intracellular ice crystal formation is **lethal** — it punctures organelles and membranes.

**Solution:** Use **Cryoprotective Agents (CPAs):**
- **DMSO (Dimethyl Sulfoxide) — 10% v/v** — penetrates cells, lowers freezing point, reduces ice nucleation.
- **Glycerol — 5–10%** — alternative CPA.
- **Methanol (some protocols)** — for specific cryoprotection.

**Controlled-Rate Freezing Profile:**
1. **0°C → -4°C:** Equilibration (seeding step).
2. **-4°C → -30°C:** Slow at **-1°C/min** — allows cellular dehydration, prevents intracellular ice.
3. **-30°C → -80°C:** Faster rate acceptable.
4. **-80°C → -196°C:** Transfer to liquid nitrogen for long-term storage.

#### **Pillar 5 — Thawing & Revitalization**

Thawing must be **rapid (~1–2 minutes at 37°C)** to prevent recrystallization of intracellular ice. Immediately dilute the CPA-containing medium 1:10 to reduce DMSO toxicity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** A freeze-thaw cycle causes a viability drop of **5–15%**. Paternity/master stocks must NEVER be thawed into working stocks directly — use the **Master Bank → Working Bank → Experimental Use** hierarchy.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Variable Definitions | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cell Density (Hemocytometer)** | $$\text{Cells/mL} = \frac{\text{Average count per square} \times \text{Dilution factor} \times 10^{4}}{\text{Number of squares counted}}$$ | $10^4$ = chamber depth-area constant for Neubauer | Cells·mL⁻¹ |
| **Total Cell Number** | $$\text{Total} = \text{Cells/mL} \times \text{Volume of suspension (mL)}$$ | — | Cells |
| **Population Doubling Time (PDT)** | $$\text{PDT} = \frac{\text{Duration (h)}}{\text{Number of doublings}}$$ | Doublings = $\log_2(\text{Final}/\text{Initial})$ | Hours |
| **Number of Doublings per Passage** | $$\text{Doublings} = \log_2(N)$$ | N = split ratio (for 1:N split) | Dimensionless |
| **Split Ratio Selection** | $$\text{Seeding Density} = \frac{\text{Total cells harvested}}{\text{Desired split factor}}$$ | — | Cells/cm² |
| **Viability (% Trypan Blue exclusion)** | $$\% \text{Viability} = \frac{\text{Viable cells}}{\text{Total cells}} \times 100$$ | — | Percentage (%) |
| **Cryoprotectant Concentration** | $$\text{DMSO}_{\text{final}} = \text{Stock}\% \times \frac{V_{\text{stock}}}{V_{\text{stock}} + V_{\text{medium}}}$$ | — | % v/v |
| **Doubling Time (Monod-derived)** | $$\ln 2 \times \frac{1}{\mu_{\max}}$$ | $\mu_{\max}$ = max specific growth rate (h⁻¹) | Hours |
| **Specific Growth Rate** | $$\mu = \frac{\ln(N_t / N_0)}{t}$$ | $N_t$ = cells at time t; $N_0$ = initial | h⁻¹ |
| **Freezing Rate Profile** | $$\frac{dT}{dt} = -1°C/\text{min}$$ | Constant slow rate from 0 to -30°C | °C·min⁻¹ |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Biotechnology Industries

- **Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing:** Master Cell Banks (MCB) and Working Cell Banks (WCB) are the legal, regulatory foundation of every approved mAb (e.g., Adalimumab, Rituximab) and viral vaccine (e.g., Vero cells for polio vaccine). Regulatory compliance is governed by **ICH Q5A, Q5D, and Q6B** guidelines.
- **Regenerative Medicine:** Maintenance of induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs) under feeder-free, defined media conditions is the cornerstone of autologous cell therapy.
- **CRISPR Genome Editing:** Maintaining isogenic cell populations post-edit requires rigorous single-cell cloning and cryobanking.
- **Tissue Engineering:** Maintenance of scaffolds + cell constructs (3D cultures) requires perfusion bioreactor systems rather than static flasks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Cell Counting by Hemocytometer (Neubauer Chamber)

A hemocytometer is a thick glass slide with a precision-etched counting chamber. The standard chamber has 25 large squares, each subdivided into 16 small squares, with a chamber depth of **0.1 mm** and a total ruled area of **1 mm²**.

**Given:**
- Cell suspension diluted 1:1 with 0.4% Trypan Blue.
- Counted 4 large corner squares (each = 1 mm²).
- Counts: 82, 95, 78, 89 viable cells.
- Total volume of diluted sample = 1 mL.

**Step 1 — Compute the average count per large square.**

$$\text{Average} = \frac{82 + 95 + 78 + 89}{4} = \frac{344}{4} = 86 \text{ cells/square}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the dilution factor.**

Since cells were diluted 1:1 with Trypan Blue, the original concentration is 2× the diluted count.

**Step 3 — Apply the chamber geometric constant.**

The volume above 1 large square = $1 \text{ mm}^2 \times 0.1 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ mm}^3 = 10^{-4} \text{ mL}$.

To get cells per mL, multiply by $10^4$.

**Step 4 — Combine all factors.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cells/mL} &= \text{Average} \times \text{Dilution factor} \times 10^{4} \\
&= 86 \times 2 \times 10^{4} \\
&= 1.72 \times 10^{6} \text{ cells/mL}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Total cells in 1 mL suspension.**

$$\text{Total} = 1.72 \times 10^{6} \text{ cells/mL} \times 1 \text{ mL} = 1.72 \times 10^{6} \text{ cells}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** Hemocytometer math reduces to: (Mean count) × (dilution) × (chamber constant) → cells/mL. The $10^4$ factor converts the $0.1$ mm³ chamber volume into 1 mL.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Population Doubling Time (PDT)

A researcher seeds **2.0 × 10⁴ cells** in a T25 flask. After **72 hours**, a hemocytometer count reveals **3.2 × 10⁵ cells**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the number of population doublings.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Doublings} &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) \\
&= \log_2\!\left(\frac{3.2 \times 10^{5}}{2.0 \times 10^{4}}\right) \\
&= \log_2(16) \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute PDT.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PDT} &= \frac{\text{Duration (h)}}{\text{Doublings}} \\
&= \frac{72 \text{ h}}{4} \\
&= 18 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-validate using the specific growth rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{\ln(N_t / N_0)}{t} = \frac{\ln(16)}{72} = \frac{2.7726}{72} = 0.0385 \text{ h}^{-1} \\
\text{PDT} &= \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.6931}{0.0385} = 18 \text{ h} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students confuse $\log_2$ with $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$. For PDT, **always use base-2 logarithm** because "doubling" is base 2 by definition.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Cryopreservation DMSO Dilution

You have **0.5 mL of cell pellet** (suspended in growth medium) and want to add DMSO to a final concentration of **10% v/v**.

**Step 1 — Define variables.**
- $V_{\text{cells+medium}} = 0.5$ mL
- $C_{\text{final}} = 10\%$
- $C_{\text{stock}} = 100\%$ (pure DMSO)

**Step 2 — Apply the dilution equation.**

$$C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$$

$$100\% \times V_{\text{DMSO}} = 10\% \times V_{\text{final}}$$

**Step 3 — Express $V_{\text{final}}$.**

$$V_{\text{final}} = V_{\text{cells+medium}} + V_{\text{DMSO}} = 0.5 + V_{\text{DMSO}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve.**

$$\begin{aligned}
100 \times V_{\text{DMSO}} &= 10 \times (0.5 + V_{\text{DMSO}}) \\
100 V_{\text{DMSO}} &= 5 + 10 V_{\text{DMSO}} \\
90 V_{\text{DMSO}} &= 5 \\
V_{\text{DMSO}} &= 0.0556 \text{ mL} \approx 55.6 \mu\text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verify.**

$$V_{\text{final}} = 0.5 + 0.0556 = 0.5556 \text{ mL}$$
$$\frac{0.0556}{0.5556} = 0.10 = 10\% \;\;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Maintenance Decision Tree

| Observation | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| Medium turns yellow within 24 h | High metabolic rate, bacterial contamination | Check pH, test for bacteria, subculture if pH ok |
| Granular appearance under microscope | Precipitated salts, pH imbalance, mycoplasma | Test mycoplasma; switch to HEPES-buffered medium |
| Cells detach in sheets | Proteolytic over-digestion (trypsin too long) | Reduce trypsin exposure to 2–3 min, use TrypLE |
| Slow growth, enlarged cytoplasm | Senescence, high passage number | Thaw new vial from master bank, limit to <15 passages |
| Cloudy medium, no growth | Fungal/bacterial infection | Discard, decontaminate hood, review aseptic technique |
| Vacuolated cytoplasm | Osmotic stress, old medium, viral contamination | Replace medium, test for virus, reduce passage number |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Workflow — The Maintenance Cycle

This block-level flow diagram represents the complete cyclic protocol of mammalian cell culture maintenance as expected in a KTU 14-mark descriptive answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Thaw Frozen Vial from Master Bank] --> A1[Day 0: Seed into T25 Flask with Pre-warmed Medium]
    A1 --> A2[Day 1: Check Attachment and Morphology under Inverted Microscope]
    A2 --> A3{Confluence Reached 80 to 95 percent}
    A3 -- NO --> A4[Replace 70 to 100 percent Spent Medium]
    A4 --> A2
    A3 -- YES --> A5[Subculture: Wash with PBS, Add Trypsin EDTA, Incubate 3 to 5 min at 37C]
    A5 --> A6[Inactivate Trypsin with Serum Containing Medium]
    A6 --> A7[Centrifuge 200 x g for 5 min, Resuspend in Fresh Medium]
    A7 --> A8[Perform Cell Count and Viability Trypan Blue]
    A8 --> A9{Split Decision: 1 to 5 or 1 to 10}
    A9 --> A10[Seed New Flasks at 1 to 5 times 10 to the 4 cells per sq cm]
    A9 --> A11[Cryopreserve 1 to 2 million cells per vial in 10 percent DMSO]
    A9 --> A12[Dispose Excess Biohazard Waste]
    A10 --> A2
    A11 --> A13[Controlled Rate Freezing: minus 1C per min to minus 80C, then LN2 at minus 196C]
    A12 --> A14[Document Passage Number, Date, Operator in Logbook]
    A13 --> A14
    A14 --> A2
```

### 4.2 Cryopreservation & Thawing Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Confluent Healthy Culture] --> B1[Trypsinize and Resuspend]
    B1 --> B2[Centrifuge and Count Cells]
    B2 --> B3[Resuspend at 1 times 10 to the 6 cells per mL in Freezing Medium]
    B3 --> B4[Add 10 percent DMSO Dropwise on Ice]
    B4 --> B5[Aliquot 1 mL into Cryovials]
    B5 --> B6[Place in Mr Frosty Container]
    B6 --> B7[Store at minus 80C Overnight: minus 1C per min Rate]
    B7 --> B8[Transfer to Liquid Nitrogen Tank: minus 196C]
    B8 --> B9[Long Term Storage Master Bank]
    B9 --> B10[Retrieval: Rapid Thaw at 37C for 1 to 2 min]
    B10 --> B11[Dilute 1 to 10 in Pre warmed Medium]
    B11 --> B12[Centrifuge to Remove DMSO]
    B12 --> B13[Resuspend and Seed into T25 Flask]
    B13 --> B14[Day 1: Assess Viability and Attachment]
```

### 4.3 Contamination Detection Decision Matrix (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Routine Monitoring Trigger: Every 2 to 4 Weeks] --> C1[Visual Inspection: Medium Color and Clarity]
    C1 --> C2{Turbid or Cloudy}
    C2 -- YES --> C3[Gram Stain and Microscopy for Bacteria Fungi]
    C2 -- NO --> C4[Phase Contrast Morphology Check]
    C4 --> C5[Abnormal Granular or Vacuolated Cells]
    C5 -- YES --> C6[Mycoplasma PCR or Hoechst DNA Stain]
    C5 -- NO --> C7[Suspicion of Cross Contamination]
    C7 -- YES --> C8[STR Profiling of Cell Line]
    C7 -- NO --> C9[Culture Healthy: Continue Routine Passaging]
    C3 --> C10[Contamination Confirmed: Discard and Decontaminate]
    C6 --> C10
    C8 --> C10
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**

> Define the term "cell culture maintenance." List any four routine practices involved in maintaining an *in vitro* cell culture.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cell culture maintenance is the systematic set of aseptic bioprocess operations — including medium renewal, subculturing (passaging), contamination monitoring, and cryopreservation — performed at regular intervals to preserve the viability, phenotypic stability, and proliferative capacity of cultured cells.

**Four routine practices (any four):**
1. **Medium change/feeding** every 2–3 days to replenish glucose, glutamine, and remove toxic metabolites (lactate, ammonia).
2. **Subculturing (passaging)** at 80–95% confluence using enzymatic dissociation (Trypsin-EDTA) to prevent contact inhibition.
3. **Microscopic monitoring** daily using an inverted phase-contrast microscope to assess morphology, confluence, and signs of contamination.
4. **Cryopreservation** of early-passage stocks in **10% DMSO** and liquid nitrogen at **-196°C** for long-term banking.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**

> What is the role of DMSO in cryopreservation of animal cell cultures? Why is controlled-rate freezing preferred over direct transfer to -80°C?

**Model Answer:**

**Role of DMSO:** Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is a **cryoprotective agent (CPA)** that penetrates the cell membrane, lowers the freezing point of intracellular water, and reduces the formation of lethal intracellular ice crystals during the freezing process. It is typically used at **10% v/v** in freezing medium.

**Why controlled-rate freezing?**
- Direct transfer to -80°C causes **rapid, uncontrolled ice nucleation** → lethal intracellular ice → cell death.
- Controlled-rate freezing at **-1°C per minute** allows **cellular dehydration** to occur gradually, concentrating intracellular solutes and preventing mechanical rupture.
- After the critical -4°C to -30°C window, cells are transferred to **liquid nitrogen at -196°C** for indefinite storage.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Cell Counting, Subculture & PDT Calculations**

**[KTU University Exam – Model, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** Describe the **hemocytometer-based cell counting procedure** using the Trypan Blue exclusion method. Include the formula, the dilution logic, and the chamber geometry. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A researcher seeds **5 × 10⁴ cells/mL** in a suspension culture. After **96 hours**, the cell density reaches **8 × 10⁵ cells/mL**. Calculate the **(i)** specific growth rate (μ), **(ii)** population doubling time (PDT), and **(iii)** comment on the suitability of the culture for scale-up. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Hemocytometer Procedure (7 Marks):**

1. **Sample preparation:** Mix 100 μL of cell suspension with 100 μL of **0.4% Trypan Blue** (1:1 dilution). Trypan Blue is excluded by live cells with intact membranes; dead cells stain blue.

2. **Loading the chamber:** Clean the hemocytometer with 70% ethanol. Place the coverslip; load 10–20 μL of the stained cell suspension at the edge of the coverslip. Capillary action draws the liquid under.

3. **Counting:** Place the chamber under a phase-contrast microscope (10× objective). Count cells in the **4 large corner squares** of the Neubauer grid (each square = 1 mm², depth = 0.1 mm).

4. **Formula (with chamber constant $10^4$):**

$$\text{Cells/mL} = \frac{\text{Average viable count per square} \times \text{Dilution factor} \times 10^{4}}{\text{Number of squares counted}}$$

5. **Viability calculation:**

$$\% \text{Viability} = \frac{\text{Unstained cells}}{\text{Total cells (stained + unstained)}} \times 100$$

6. **Acceptance criterion:** Only cultures with **≥ 90% viability** should be used for subculture or cryopreservation.

---

**Part (b) — Growth Kinetics (7 Marks):**

**(i) Specific Growth Rate (μ):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{\ln(N_t / N_0)}{t} \\
&= \frac{\ln\!\left(\frac{8 \times 10^{5}}{5 \times 10^{4}}\right)}{96} \\
&= \frac{\ln(16)}{96} \\
&= \frac{2.7726}{96} \\
&= 0.0289 \text{ h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Population Doubling Time (PDT):**

$$\text{PDT} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.6931}{0.0289} \approx 24 \text{ hours}$$

**Verification via doublings:**

$$\text{Doublings} = \log_2(16) = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PDT} = \frac{96}{4} = 24 \text{ h} \;\;\checkmark$$

**(iii) Scale-up suitability:**

- A PDT of **24 hours** indicates a healthy, moderately fast-growing culture.
- A 16-fold (4-doubling) increase over 4 days is consistent with a culture in **mid-exponential phase**.
- **Suitable for scale-up** if viability ≥ 90%, mycoplasma-free, and metabolic profile (glucose consumption, lactate production) is stable.
- For industrial bioreactor scale-up, μ should be reproducible across 10–100 L working volumes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Cryopreservation, Contamination & Maintenance Protocols**

**[KTU University Exam – Model, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **principle and protocol of cryopreservation** of animal cell cultures. Explain the role of DMSO and the rationale for the controlled-rate freezing profile. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** List the **common types of contamination** in animal cell culture. Describe **mycoplasma detection by PCR** and state why antibiotic-free media is recommended for routine maintenance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Cryopreservation Principle & Protocol (7 Marks):**

**Principle:** Cryopreservation preserves cells by reducing their temperature below the glass transition point (~-130°C) so that all biochemical and metabolic reactions cease. Cells can be stored indefinitely at **-196°C in liquid nitrogen**.

**Key challenges:**
- **Intracellular ice nucleation** is lethal → punctures membranes and organelles.
- **Osmotic shock** during freezing and thawing → cell volume changes cause apoptosis.

**Protocol (8 steps):**
1. **Select culture:** Use healthy, mid-log phase cells with **≥ 90% viability**.
2. **Harvest:** Trypsinize (adherent) or collect by centrifugation (suspension).
3. **Count and resuspend:** Adjust to **1 × 10⁶ cells/mL** in freezing medium (e.g., 90% FBS + 10% DMSO, or 70% medium + 20% FBS + 10% DMSO).
4. **Aliquot:** Dispense **1 mL** into **cryogenic vials** (Nunc/Corning) — pre-labeled with cell line, passage, date, operator.
5. **Slow freezing:** Place vials in a **Mr. Frosty container** with isopropanol, then transfer to a **-80°C freezer** overnight. This achieves a **-1°C per minute cooling rate**.
6. **Transfer to LN₂:** After 18–24 hours at -80°C, transfer to **liquid nitrogen storage tank** (vapor phase, -196°C).
7. **Documentation:** Record location (tank, rack, box, position) in the laboratory cryobank logbook.
8. **Master-Working Bank hierarchy:** Preserve a **Master Cell Bank (MCB)** untouched; generate **Working Cell Banks (WCB)** for routine use to limit freeze-thaw cycles of the original stock.

**Role of DMSO:**
- Penetrates cell membrane → lowers intracellular freezing point.
- Displaces water → reduces ice formation.
- Used at **5–10% v/v**; concentrations >10% are cytotoxic.

**Why -1°C/min?**
- Allows **cellular dehydration** by extracellular water migration.
- Prevents intracellular ice formation.
- This rate has been empirically validated (Mazur's two-factor hypothesis, 1984).

---

**Part (b) — Contamination & Detection (7 Marks):**

**Common contamination types:**
1. **Bacterial** (E. coli, Mycoplasma) — turbidity, pH drop, granulation.
2. **Fungal/Yeast** (Candida, Aspergillus) — hyphae, cloudy medium.
3. **Mycoplasma** — most insidious; ~0.3 μm, no turbidity, alters cell metabolism.
4. **Viral** — usually subclinical; detected by cytopathic effect (CPE) or EM.
5. **Cross-contamination** — HeLa cells contaminating other lines (documented in 18–36% of cell lines historically — Nelson-Rees et al., 1981).

**Mycoplasma detection by PCR:**
- Amplify the conserved **16S–23S rRNA intergenic spacer** region of the mycoplasma genome.
- Use genus-specific primers (e.g., **MGSO/GPO-1** set).
- **Procedure:** Extract DNA from culture supernatant (mycoplasmas are extracellular or membrane-bound) → PCR amplification → agarose gel electrophoresis → positive band at **~280 bp** indicates contamination.
- **Sensitivity:** Detects <10 mycoplasma cells per mL — far superior to culture-based assays.
- **Alternative methods:** Hoechst 33258 DNA staining, MycoAlert luminescence assay, ELISA.

**Why antibiotic-free media is recommended:**
1. Antibiotics **mask low-level contamination**, allowing chronic infections to persist undetected.
2. They **select for resistant strains** (e.g., mycoplasma resistant to most routine antibiotics).
3. Antibiotics like penicillin/streptomycin **alter cellular gene expression, mitochondrial function, and proliferation rate** — confounding experimental results.
4. Regulatory bodies (FDA, EMA) require antibiotic-free cell banks for biologics manufacturing.
5. Good **aseptic technique** is the only sustainable defense.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Confusing $\log_2$ with $\log_{10}$ in PDT calculations → full 2-mark deduction.
> - ❌ Writing "cells are stored at -80°C indefinitely" — incorrect; long-term is **-196°C LN₂**; -80°C is for short-term only (~6 months).
> - ❌ Stating "antibiotics prevent contamination" — they **prevent bacterial growth, not contamination**; aseptic technique is the gold standard.
> - ❌ Skipping the dilution factor in hemocytometer calculations — examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> - ❌ Describing thawing as "slow" — it must be **rapid (1–2 min at 37°C)** to prevent recrystallization.
> - ❌ Omitting the **viability threshold (≥ 90%)** in any cell culture answer → examiners mark as incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Maintenance = 5 pillars:** Feeding, Subculturing, Monitoring, Contamination control, Cryopreservation.
- ✅ **Subculture at 80–95% confluence** to prevent contact inhibition.
- ✅ **Split ratios:** 1:2 (primary) to 1:20 (continuous lines).
- ✅ **Trypsin-EDTA (0.25%)** for 3–5 min at 37°C for adherent cell dissociation.
- ✅ **Neutralize trypsin** with serum-containing medium to prevent over-digestion.
- ✅ **Hemocytometer formula:** Cells/mL = Average × Dilution × $10^4$ / Squares.
- ✅ **Viability ≥ 90%** is the acceptance criterion for downstream use.
- ✅ **PDT = ln 2 / μ** = 72 h / doublings (for 72-h interval).
- ✅ **Cryopreservation:** 10% DMSO, **-1°C/min** to -80°C, then -196°C LN₂.
- ✅ **Master Bank → Working Bank** hierarchy prevents exhaustion of original stock.
- ✅ **Thaw rapidly** at 37°C for 1–2 min, then dilute 1:10 to reduce DMSO toxicity.
- ✅ **Mycoplasma** is the most insidious contaminant; screen every 1–3 months by **PCR or Hoechst**.
- ✅ **Antibiotics mask, not prevent, contamination** — never substitute for aseptic technique.
- ✅ **pH indicator:** Phenol Red = yellow (acidic, high lactate) → red (healthy) → purple (alkaline, NH₃ buildup).
- ✅ **Incubator conditions:** **37°C, 5% CO₂, ~95% humidity** (mammalian).
- ✅ **Cell line authentication** via STR profiling is mandatory for publication-grade work.
- ✅ **Passage number must be recorded**; many lines undergo senescence or transformation after 15–30 passages (Hayflick limit ~50 for primary fibroblasts).
- ✅ **Document everything:** date, passage, operator, medium lot, observations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **cell line** is defined as a permanently established cell culture that has undergone genetic transformation (either spontaneous or induced) to acquire the ability to proliferate indefinitely *in vitro*. The term originates from the first successful propagation of the **L929 mouse fibroblast cell line** by William Earle in 1943 at the National Cancer Institute.

According to KTU 2024 scheme conventions, the hierarchy of cultured cells is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hierarchical Terminology (Must Know for Exams)**
> - **Primary Culture (P0):** Cells freshly isolated from tissue, not yet subcultured.
> - **Primary Cell Line:** Cells that have been subcultured once (Passage 1, P1).
> - **Finite Cell Line (Diploid Strain):** Cells with a limited lifespan (~20–80 population doublings) that retain original chromosomal karyotype.
> - **Continuous (Established/Infinite) Cell Line:** Cells capable of indefinite proliferation, usually aneuploid or heteroploid with chromosomal alterations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of cell lines as **"biological factories"** or a **"continuously running library of cells."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Photocopy Machine**
> Imagine you have a single original photograph (the primary tissue). You place it on a photocopy machine and make one copy (Passage 1). Now you can copy the copy (Passage 2), and so on. As long as you maintain the machine (proper media, temperature, sterile conditions), you can make hundreds of copies. A **finite cell line** is like an old photocopy machine that wears out after ~50–80 copies and starts producing smudged images. A **continuous cell line** is a machine that has been upgraded (via viral transformation or spontaneous mutation) to copy indefinitely, though the resulting images (chromosomes) may show slight distortions (aneuploidy).

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics

The standard doubling time of most mammalian cell lines at **37 °C** with **5% CO₂** ranges between **18–24 hours**. The cell density at which contact inhibition triggers subculturing is typically **~80–90% confluence**.

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Optimal pH | 7.2 – 7.4 |
| CO₂ tension | 5% |
| Incubation temperature | 37 °C ± 0.5 °C |
| Osmolality | 280 – 320 mOsm/kg |
| Humidity | 95 – 98% relative |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cell Lines

### 2.1.1 Based on Lifespan

1. **Finite (Diploid) Cell Lines**
   - Limited lifespan (~20–80 population doublings)
   - Maintain contact inhibition
   - Anchorage-dependent
   - Retain diploid karyotype
   - Example: **WI-38** (human embryonic lung fibroblasts), **MRC-5**

2. **Continuous (Established) Cell Lines**
   - Unlimited proliferation potential
   - Often anchorage-independent
   - Reduced or absent contact inhibition
   - Aneuploid karyotype
   - Examples: **HeLa** (cervical cancer), **Vero** (African green monkey kidney), **CHO** (Chinese Hamster Ovary), **HEK 293**

### 2.1.2 Based on Growth Morphology

- **Adherent cell lines** – grow attached to a substrate (e.g., fibroblasts, epithelial cells)
- **Suspension cell lines** – free-floating in medium (e.g., hybridomas, hematopoietic cells)

### 2.1.3 Based on Species Origin

- Human, Mouse, Rat, Hamster, Monkey, Insect (Sf9, Sf21), Fish (ZF4), Avian

## 2.2 Transformation Mechanisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanisms of Spontaneous vs Induced Immortalization**
> - **Spontaneous:** Rare event, e.g., HeLa from cervical adenocarcinoma.
> - **Induced:**
>   - **Chemical:** 3-Methylcholanthrene, benzopyrene
>   - **Viral:** SV40 large T-antigen, EBV (Epstein-Barr Virus), HPV E6/E7
>   - **Genetic:** Overexpression of telomerase (hTERT), c-Myc, Ras oncogenes
>   - **Radiation:** UV, X-rays

The hallmark molecular feature of immortalization is the **reactivation of telomerase** to maintain telomere length beyond the Hayflick limit.

## 2.3 Routine Maintenance Protocol

Routine maintenance involves six interconnected pillars:

### Pillar 1: Media and Supplements
- **Basal media:** DMEM, RPMI-1640, MEM, Ham's F-12, IMDM
- **Serum:** Typically **5–20% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS)** — provides growth factors, hormones, attachment factors, and pH buffering
- **Antibiotics:** Penicillin (100 U/mL) + Streptomycin (100 µg/mL) — optional, never as substitute for asepsis
- **pH indicator:** Phenol red (yellow at acidic, pink at alkaline, red-orange at optimal 7.4)

### Pillar 2: Subculturing (Passaging)

The process involves:
1. **Observation** under inverted phase-contrast microscope
2. **Removal** of spent medium
3. **Washing** with PBS (Phosphate Buffered Saline) to remove serum (serum inhibits trypsin)
4. **Dissociation** using Trypsin-EDTA (0.25% trypsin + 0.02% EDTA)
   - Trypsin cleaves peptide bonds at C-terminal of Lys/Arg
   - EDTA chelates Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ essential for cell adhesion
5. **Neutralization** with serum-containing medium
6. **Centrifugation** at ~1000 rpm for 5 minutes
7. **Resuspension** and seeding at appropriate split ratio (typically 1:2 to 1:10)

### Pillar 3: Cryopreservation

> [!NOTE]
> **Cryopreservation Protocol (Long-term Storage)**
> - Cryoprotectant: **10% Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO)** or 10% glycerol
> - Cooling rate: **–1 °C/min** (controlled-rate freezer) or **Mr. Frosty™** container at **–80 °C** overnight
> - Long-term storage: **Liquid nitrogen vapor phase (–150 °C to –196 °C)**
> - Avoid **–20 °C** for cryopreservation due to lethal ice crystal formation
> - Rule: **Cool slow, warm fast**

### Pillar 4: Contamination Monitoring
- **Mycoplasma testing** — weekly or per lot (PCR, Hoechst stain, MycoAlert kit)
- **Microbial sterility checks** — turbidity in media, Gram staining
- **Cross-contamination detection** — DNA fingerprinting (STR profiling for human lines), isozyme analysis

### Pillar 5: Cell Authentication

For human cell lines, the **ATCC Standards for Cell Line Authentication** mandate:
- **Short Tandem Repeat (STR) profiling** of 8–9 loci (TH01, vWA, D5S818, D13S317, D7S820, D16S539, TPOX, CSF1PO, Amelogenin)
- Comparison with ATCC, DSMZ, or Cellosaurus databases

### Pillar 6: Passage Number Documentation

Maintain a **passage log** to track:
- Cell line ID, passage number, date, operator, media lot, viability, morphology

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Population Doubling Time (PDT) | $PDT = \frac{t \cdot \ln(2)}{\ln(N_t / N_0)}$ | $t$ = time interval, $N_0$ = initial cells, $N_t$ = final cells |
| Cell Viability (Trypan Blue) | $\% \text{ Viability} = \frac{\text{Live cells}}{\text{Total cells}} \times 100$ | Acceptable threshold: $\geq$ 90% |
| Split Ratio | $\text{SR} = \frac{\text{Cells harvested}}{\text{Cells seeded}}$ | Typical: 1:4 to 1:10 |
| Cell Density for Seeding | $\text{Seeding density} = \frac{\text{Total cells}}{\text{Surface area (cm}^2\text{)}}$ | 5,000–10,000 cells/cm² (adherent) |
| Cryopreservation Cell Density | $1 \times 10^6$ to $1 \times 10^7$ cells/mL | In 90% medium + 10% DMSO |
| Hayflick Limit | ~50–70 population doublings | For normal diploid human fibroblasts |
| Generation Number | $n = \log_2(\text{Passage ratio} \times \text{Dilution factor})$ | Estimates true population doublings |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vaccine production:** Vero cells for polio, rabies, COVID-19 vaccines
- **Monoclonal antibody production:** CHO and hybridoma (NS0, Sp2/0) cell lines
- **Recombinant protein therapeutics:** HEK 293 for transient transfection
- **Drug screening:** High-throughput assays on HepG2, A549, MCF-7
- **Tissue engineering & regenerative medicine**
- **3D cell culture and organoid development**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Population Doubling Time (PDT)

**Problem Statement:** A researcher subcultures a HeLa cell line. After 24 hours, the cell count rises from $5 \times 10^4$ cells/mL to $2 \times 10^5$ cells/mL. Calculate the Population Doubling Time.

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**
Let $N_0$ be the initial cell density and $N_t$ be the final cell density after time $t$ (in hours). Cell growth in log phase follows exponential kinetics:

$$N_t = N_0 \cdot 2^{n}$$

where $n$ is the number of population doublings.

**Step 2 — Solve for $n$ (number of doublings).**

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln(N_t) = \ln(N_0) + n \cdot \ln(2)$$

$$n = \frac{\ln(N_t / N_0)}{\ln(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$N_0 = 5 \times 10^4 \text{ cells/mL}, \quad N_t = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ cells/mL}, \quad t = 24 \text{ h}$$

$$\frac{N_t}{N_0} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{5 \times 10^4} = 4$$

$$n = \frac{\ln(4)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{1.3863}{0.6931} = 2 \text{ doublings}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the PDT formula.**

Since $n$ doublings occur in $t$ hours, the time for one doubling is:

$$PDT = \frac{t}{n} = \frac{t \cdot \ln(2)}{\ln(N_t / N_0)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute PDT.**

$$PDT = \frac{24 \times \ln(2)}{\ln(4)} = \frac{24 \times 0.6931}{1.3863} = \frac{16.6344}{1.3863} = 12 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** HeLa cells double every **12 hours** under standard conditions — this falls in the biologically realistic range (typical HeLa PDT = 18–24 h; aggressive log-phase growth can approach 12 h).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Split Ratio & Seeding Density

**Problem Statement:** You have a confluent T75 flask (surface area = 75 cm²) with $7.5 \times 10^6$ HeLa cells. You wish to subculture at a split ratio of 1:5. How many cells will you seed per mL of fresh medium, and how much medium should you use?

**Step 1 — Determine cells to be seeded.**

$$\text{Seeded cells} = \frac{\text{Total harvested cells}}{\text{Split ratio denominator}}$$

$$\text{Seeded cells} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^6}{5} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ cells per new flask}$$

**Step 2 — Determine cells per cm².**

$$\text{Cells per cm}^2 = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{75} = 2 \times 10^4 \text{ cells/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Determine volume of medium.**

Standard recommendation: **0.2–0.3 mL medium per cm²** for adherent cells.

$$V_{\text{medium}} = 0.2 \times 75 = 15 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 4 — Compute final seeding concentration.**

$$C_{\text{seed}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ cells}}{15 \text{ mL}} = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ cells/mL}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Cell Lines

| Property | HeLa | CHO | Vero | HEK 293 | WI-38 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Origin | Human cervical cancer | Chinese hamster ovary | Monkey kidney | Human embryonic kidney | Human fetal lung |
| Lifespan | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous | Finite (~50 PD) |
| Karyotype | Aneuploid | Near-diploid | Aneuploid | Aneuploid | Diploid |
| Growth mode | Adherent | Adherent / Suspension | Adherent | Adherent | Adherent |
| Doubling time | 18–24 h | 12–14 h | 18–24 h | 24–30 h | 24–48 h |
| Special use | Cancer biology, transfection | Recombinant protein | Vaccine production | Transfection, viral vectors | Aging research |
| Serum requirement | 10% FBS | 5–10% FBS | 5–10% FBS | 10% FBS | 10–15% FBS |

---
