# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow: Routine Maintenance Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Cryovial from LN2 storage] --> B[Thaw at 37°C in water bath]
    B --> C[Transfer to 9 mL pre-warmed medium]
    C --> D[Centrifuge 1000 rpm, 5 min]
    D --> E[Resuspend pellet, seed in T25 flask]
    E --> F[Incubate at 37°C, 5% CO2]
    F --> G{Monitor confluence}
    G -->|Less than 80%| H[Refresh medium every 2-3 days]
    G -->|More than 80%| I[Subculture / Passage]
    I --> J[Wash with PBS]
    J --> K[Add Trypsin-EDTA]
    K --> L[Neutralize with serum medium]
    L --> M[Centrifuge and resuspend]
    M --> N{Decision branch}
    N -->|Continue culture| E
    N -->|Stock production| O[Freeze in 10% DMSO]
    N -->|Authentication needed| P[STR profiling]
    N -->|Mycoplasma testing| Q[PCR or Hoechst stain]
    O --> R[Store in liquid nitrogen]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Cell Line Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cultured Cells] --> B[Primary Culture P0]
    B --> C[Finite Cell Line]
    B --> D[Continuous Cell Line]

    C --> C3[Limited PD ~20-80]
    C --> C4[Example WI-38, MRC-5]

    D --> D2[Loss of contact inhibition]
    D --> D3[Unlimited proliferation]

```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Cryopreservation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Log phase culture] --> B[Trypsinization]
    B --> C[Cell count and viability]
    C --> D{Viability greater than 90%}
    D -->|Yes| E[Centrifuge and resuspend]
    D -->|No| F[Discard - poor quality]
    E --> G[Add freeze medium: 90% FBS + 10% DMSO]
    G --> H[Aliquot into cryovials 1 mL]
    H --> I[Controlled rate freezing minus 1 deg C per min]
    I --> J[Transfer to LN2 vapor phase minus 150 deg C]
    J --> K[Long term storage]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a continuous cell line. Give two examples and mention one key feature.**

**Model Answer:**
A continuous (established) cell line is a population of cells that has acquired the ability to proliferate indefinitely *in vitro*, usually through spontaneous or induced transformation. It typically exhibits aneuploidy, loss of contact inhibition, and reduced serum dependence.

**Examples:** HeLa (human cervical carcinoma) and CHO (Chinese Hamster Ovary).

**Key feature:** Unlimited lifespan — can be subcultured indefinitely without entering senescence.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Why is DMSO used as a cryoprotectant in cell line preservation?**

**Model Answer:**
Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is a cryoprotectant that prevents intracellular ice crystal formation during freezing. At **10% v/v concentration**, it lowers the freezing point of the cytoplasm, allowing controlled dehydration and minimizing mechanical damage to cellular membranes and organelles. It also penetrates cells rapidly due to its low molecular weight (78.13 g/mol) and amphipathic nature.

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Classify cell lines based on their lifespan and growth characteristics. Discuss the salient features of finite and continuous cell lines with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the step-by-step protocol for routine subculturing (passaging) of an adherent mammalian cell line. Include the role of Trypsin-EDTA and split ratio. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification and Features**

Cell lines are classified based on lifespan and growth behavior as follows:

**1. Finite (Diploid) Cell Lines:**
- Limited lifespan (~20–80 population doublings)
- Maintain diploid karyotype (chromosome number intact)
- Exhibit contact inhibition
- Anchorage-dependent
- Enter senescence via telomere shortening (Hayflick limit)
- Examples: WI-38, MRC-5, IMR-90

**2. Continuous (Established) Cell Lines:**
- Unlimited proliferation potential
- Aneuploid or heteroploid karyotype
- Loss of contact inhibition
- May be anchorage-independent
- Genetically transformed (spontaneously, virally, or chemically)
- Examples: HeLa, Vero, CHO, HEK 293, BHK-21

**Key Differences Tabulated:**

| Feature | Finite Line | Continuous Line |
|---|---|---|
| Lifespan | Limited | Unlimited |
| Karyotype | Diploid | Aneuploid |
| Contact inhibition | Yes | Reduced/absent |
| Tumorigenicity | No | Often yes |
| Serum dependence | Higher | Lower |

**(b) Subculturing Protocol for Adherent Cell Lines:**

**Step 1 — Pre-warm:** Bring media, PBS, and Trypsin-EDTA to 37 °C.

**Step 2 — Visual inspection:** Observe under inverted phase-contrast microscope for confluence (target: 80–90%).

**Step 3 — Wash:** Aspirate spent medium and wash once with PBS (Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺-free) to remove residual serum that inhibits trypsin.

**Step 4 — Dissociation:** Add 2–3 mL of 0.25% Trypsin-EDTA. Incubate at 37 °C for 2–5 minutes until cells detach (observed as rounded, refractile cells under microscope).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Trypsin-EDTA?**
> - **Trypsin** — a serine protease cleaving peptide bonds at the C-terminal of lysine and arginine residues, releasing cells from the substratum.
> - **EDTA** — chelates divalent cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) essential for cadherin-mediated cell-cell adhesion and integrin attachment.

**Step 5 — Neutralization:** Add 5–7 mL of serum-containing medium to inactivate trypsin.

**Step 6 — Centrifugation:** Spin at ~1000 rpm (200 × g) for 5 minutes.

**Step 7 — Resuspension:** Aspirate supernatant; resuspend pellet in fresh medium.

**Step 8 — Counting and Seeding:** Count using hemocytometer; seed at appropriate density. Common split ratios: 1:4 to 1:10.

**Step 9 — Incubation:** Label with passage number, date, operator; place in CO₂ incubator.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the principles and protocol of cryopreservation of cell lines. Explain the role of cryoprotectants and controlled-rate freezing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is mycoplasma contamination? Describe two detection methods and the steps to eradicate contamination in a cell culture laboratory. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Cryopreservation of Cell Lines**

**Principle:**
Cryopreservation aims to maintain cell viability over long periods by arresting all biochemical reactions at ultra-low temperatures. The critical challenge is preventing **intracellular ice crystal formation**, which causes mechanical disruption of organelles and membranes.

**Cryoprotectants:**
- **Penetrating (intracellular):** DMSO (10%), glycerol (10%) — reduce ice formation by increasing solute concentration inside cells.
- **Non-penetrating (extracellular):** Sucrose, trehalose, hydroxyethyl starch — create osmotic gradient promoting cellular dehydration.

**Protocol:**

**Step 1:** Harvest log-phase cells via trypsinization (adherent) or direct collection (suspension).

**Step 2:** Resuspend at **1 × 10⁶ – 1 × 10⁷ cells/mL** in freezing medium (90% FBS + 10% DMSO, or 95% medium + 5% DMSO + 10% FBS).

**Step 3:** Transfer 1 mL aliquots into labeled cryovials (label: cell line ID, passage, date, operator).

**Step 4:** Controlled-rate freezing at **–1 °C/min**:
- Use programmable freezer, or
- Use **Mr. Frosty™** container (filled with isopropanol) at **–80 °C** for 4–24 h
- Avoid placing vials directly at –20 °C (lethal ice crystals)

**Step 5:** Transfer vials to **liquid nitrogen vapor phase (–150 °C)** for intermediate storage or **liquid phase (–196 °C)** for long-term storage.

**Step 6:** Maintain a cryostorage log.

**Thawing:** Rapidly thaw in 37 °C water bath (~2 min), immediately dilute in pre-warmed medium, centrifuge to remove DMSO, and reseed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Cryopreservation: "Cool slow, warm fast"**

---

**(b) Mycoplasma Contamination**

**Definition:**
Mycoplasmas are the smallest (0.3–0.8 µm) cell-wall-free prokaryotes belonging to the class Mollicutes. They are the most frequent and most damaging contaminants in cell culture because they pass through standard 0.22 µm filters and do not cause visible turbidity.

**Effects:**
- Alter cell metabolism, growth rate, and morphology
- Induce chromosomal aberrations
- Modify gene expression patterns
- Compromise experimental reproducibility

**Detection Methods:**

**1. PCR-based Detection (Most Widely Used):**
- Amplifies conserved 16S rRNA or *16S-23S* intergenic spacer regions
- High sensitivity (detects <10 organisms)
- Specific kits: **MycoAlert**, VenorGeM, PCR Mycoplasma Detection Kit
- Result interpretation: presence of band at expected size = positive

**2. DNA Fluorescence Staining (Hoechst 33258):**
- Cells are fixed, stained with Hoechst dye (binds A-T rich DNA)
- Visualized under fluorescence microscope
- Mycoplasma DNA appears as **extracellular punctate/granular fluorescence** outside the nucleus
- Detection limit: ~10³–10⁴ organisms

**Other methods:** Agar/broth culture (gold standard, takes 1–3 weeks), ELISA, biochemical assays (e.g., 6-MPDR enzymatic test).

**Eradication Steps:**

1. **Quarantine** contaminated cultures immediately.
2. Treat with **antibiotics** effective against mycoplasma:
   - **BM-Cyclin** (tiamulin + minocycline) — two-cycle treatment, 3 weeks
   - **Plasmocin** — 2 weeks
   - **MycoZap** (Lonza)
3. Discard irreversibly contaminated cultures and all media/bottles used.
4. **Decontaminate** hood and incubator (70% ethanol, 5% bleach).
5. **Re-test** after 2 weeks to confirm eradication.
6. Maintain preventive practices: routine screening, use of filtered media, antibiotic-free media for periodic validation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern)**
> 1. **Confusing "cell line" with "cell strain":** A *cell strain* is a sub-population cloned from a cell line having specific markers. Many students lose marks by using these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting units in calculations:** Always express PDT in **hours** and concentrations in **cells/mL or cells/cm²**.
> 3. **Improper protocol sequence:** Steps in subculturing (Wash → Trypsin → Neutralize → Centrifuge → Resuspend) must be in correct order; reversal of steps leads to zero credit for the procedure.
> 4. **Missed role of EDTA:** Many students mention trypsin only and forget that EDTA chelates Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ — examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Cryopreservation errors:** Stating –20 °C for storage, or forgetting DMSO concentration (10%), are classic mark-losing mistakes.
> 6. **No mention of aseptic technique:** Every protocol answer should be prefaced with the importance of working in a **Class II BSC (Biosafety Cabinet)** under sterile conditions.
> 7. **Mycoplasma filter pitfall:** Mycoplasma passes through 0.22 µm filters because its size (0.3 µm) is below filter cutoff — students often miss this key fact.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Cell Lines: Characteristics & Routine Maintenance**
> - ✅ Cell line ≠ Cell strain (strain is a cloned subpopulation).
> - ✅ **HeLa** — first continuous human cell line (1951, George Gey, cervical cancer).
> - ✅ **Hayflick limit** — finite lifespan (~50–70 PD) for normal somatic cells.
> - ✅ **Transformation** can be spontaneous, chemical, viral, or genetic (hTERT).
> - ✅ **Trypsin-EDTA** — Trypsin cleaves proteins; EDTA chelates Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺.
> - ✅ **Split ratio** — 1:2 to 1:10; confluence trigger at ~80–90%.
> - ✅ **PDT** formula: $PDT = \dfrac{t \cdot \ln(2)}{\ln(N_t / N_0)}$.
> - ✅ **Cryopreservation:** 10% DMSO, –1 °C/min cooling, **–196 °C** LN₂ storage, slow-cool / fast-thaw rule.
> - ✅ **Mycoplasma** — wall-less, smallest contaminant (0.3 µm), undetected by routine microscopy, detected by PCR/Hoechst.
> - ✅ **STR profiling** for human cell line authentication (8–9 loci, ATCC standards).
> - ✅ **Routine media:** DMEM/RPMI + 5–20% FBS + antibiotics; pH 7.2–7.4; 37 °C; 5% CO₂.
> - ✅ **Vero** — preferred for vaccine production; **CHO** — preferred for monoclonal antibodies.
> - ✅ Aseptic technique in **Class II BSC** is non-negotiable — mention in every protocol answer.
> - ✅ Viability ≥ 90% threshold for cryopreservation; use **trypan blue exclusion**.
> - ✅ Finite cell lines maintain diploid karyotype; continuous cell lines are usually aneuploid.

---

topics.title: Measurement of Viability and Cytotoxicity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT411 - PLANT AND ANIMAL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Viability and Cytotoxicity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Cell Viability

**Cell Viability** is formally defined as the proportion of living, metabolically active, and structurally intact cells within a given cell population at a specific point in time. It is one of the most fundamental and frequently measured parameters in any animal cell culture laboratory, bioprocess engineering, pharmaceutical screening, and tissue engineering workflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Cell viability is the quantitative measure of the number of healthy, functional cells in a sample, typically expressed as a percentage of the total cell count, and is critical for assessing culture health, bioproduct yield, drug efficacy, and toxicological responses.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, viability is treated as a **Quality Attribute (QA)** that determines whether a bioprocess is running within acceptable operating limits. Cell cultures below a viability threshold (commonly **< 80%–85%** in most industrial mammalian cell culture processes using CHO, HEK293, or hybridoma lines) are considered unsuitable for downstream processing or for drawing valid experimental conclusions.

## 1.2 Defining Cell Cytotoxicity

**Cell Cytotoxicity** refers to the quality or degree to which a chemical compound, biological agent, drug candidate, or physical treatment (e.g., radiation, temperature shock) is toxic to living cells. It is typically measured as the *opposite* of viability, although the two are conceptually distinct: a non-viable cell is *dead*, whereas a cytotoxic event may include sub-lethal damage, apoptosis, or membrane perturbation in still-living cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction between Viability and Cytotoxicity:**
> - **Viability Assay** → Answers: *"How many cells are alive?"*
> - **Cytotoxicity Assay** → Answers: *"How much damage has been caused?"*
> A cytotoxicity assay can detect *early apoptotic* cells that are still technically "viable" by membrane integrity tests, making cytotoxicity often a more sensitive indicator of cellular stress.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Aquarium Test

Imagine a rectangular fish tank holding **1000 tropical fish**. You want to know two things:
1. **How many fish are alive?** → This is **viability**. You can simply count the fish that are swimming normally versus those floating belly-up.
2. **Did the new fish food poison them?** → This is **cytotoxicity**. You might notice that even *living* fish are swimming erratically, have damaged fins, or are gasping at the surface — signs of toxicity that a simple alive/dead count misses.

In the cell culture world, the aquarium is the culture flask, the fish are your **CHO / HEK293 / HeLa cells**, the food is your **test compound**, and the experimenter is the **biotechnologist** running a cytotoxicity screen before approving a drug candidate.

## 1.4 Why Measurement is Non-Negotiable in KTU-Defined Workflows

The 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework explicitly lists cell viability and cytotoxicity measurement as a **mandatory competency** under Course Outcomes (COs) associated with bioprocess monitoring, drug discovery, and tissue engineering applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Cut-off Values Used in KTU Laboratory & Industrial Practice:**
> - **> 90%** viability → Healthy, log-phase culture; ideal for sub-culture and inoculation
> - **80% – 90%** → Acceptable for most downstream applications
> - **70% – 80%** → Borderline; sub-culturing not recommended
> - **< 70%** → Culture discard; apoptosis / necrosis dominant

## 1.5 Classification of Assay Principles

All viability and cytotoxicity assays fundamentally measure **one of three biological readouts**:

| Readout Type | Biological Basis | Representative Assays |
|--------------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Membrane Integrity** | Live cells exclude dyes; dead cells take them up | Trypan Blue, Erythrosin B, Propidium Iodide (PI) |
| **Metabolic Activity** | Live cells reduce / convert substrates via enzymes | MTT, XTT, MTS, WST-1, Resazurin, ATP-luciferase |
| **Functional / Proliferative Capacity** | Live cells can divide and form colonies | Clonogenic assay, CFU assays |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Framework — What an Assay Actually Does

Every viability / cytotoxicity assay follows a universal 4-step operational logic that you should commit to memory for KTU examinations:

1. **Sample Preparation** → Cells are harvested, washed (usually in PBS), counted (using a hemocytometer or automated cell counter), and diluted to a standardized seeding density (typically **1 × 10⁴ to 1 × 10⁶ cells/mL**, depending on the assay format — 96-well, 24-well, or 6-well plates).
2. **Treatment / Exposure** → The cell population is exposed to the test compound, drug, or stress condition for a defined period (commonly **24, 48, or 72 hours** at **37 °C in a 5% CO₂ humidified incubator**).
3. **Detection Reaction** → A specific chemical, dye, or substrate is added that interacts differently with live vs. dead / damaged cells.
4. **Quantification** → The reaction product is read using a **spectrophotometer (absorbance)**, **fluorometer (fluorescence)**, **luminometer (luminescence)**, or **flow cytometer (multi-parameter)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this 4-step framework important for KTU exams?** Because questions on "describe any viability assay" almost always expect a structured answer that walks through these four phases. Examiners reward procedural clarity.

## 2.2 The Core 'Why' Behind Each Assay Class

### 🔬 A. Dye Exclusion Assays (e.g., Trypan Blue)

**The Why:** Live cells possess an **intact, selectively permeable plasma membrane** that excludes charged dyes like trypan blue (molecular weight **960.8 g/mol**, anionic, blue). Dead cells have compromised membranes and absorb the dye, appearing dark blue under a microscope.

**The How:**
- A **0.4% (w/v)** trypan blue solution in PBS is mixed with cells in a **1:1** ratio.
- After **~2–5 minutes** of staining, cells are loaded onto a **Neubauer hemocytometer** and counted under an **inverted phase-contrast microscope** at **100× magnification**.
- **Viable cells** appear bright and refractive (do not take up dye).
- **Non-viable cells** appear dark blue (dye penetrates through broken membrane).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Trypan blue is **time-sensitive**. After ~10 minutes, *all* cells (including live ones) begin to absorb the dye due to gradual membrane deterioration. The KTU valuation key requires students to state this 2–10 minute staining window explicitly.

### 🔬 B. Metabolic Activity Assays (MTT, XTT, MTS, WST-1, Resazurin)

**The Why:** Live cells maintain active mitochondrial (and in some cases cytoplasmic) dehydrogenase enzymes. These enzymes can reduce colorless or weakly colored tetrazolium salts or redox dyes into intensely colored / fluorescent products. The intensity is proportional to the number of metabolically active cells.

#### Detailed Mechanism of MTT (the gold standard)

- **MTT** = **3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide**, a yellow tetrazolium salt.
- In live cells, mitochondrial **NADH/NADPH-dependent oxidoreductases** cleave the tetrazolium ring.
- The product is **formazan**, an insoluble purple crystalline compound with absorbance maximum at **λ ≈ 570 nm** (reference wavelength **630 nm or 690 nm** to subtract background).
- Formazan must be **solubilized** (using DMSO, acidified isopropanol, or SDS-HCl) before absorbance measurement.

#### Comparison Table of Metabolic Assays

| Assay | Substrate Type | Reduced Product | Readout | λ (nm) | Solubilization Required? |
|-------|----------------|------------------|---------|--------|--------------------------|
| **MTT** | Tetrazolium | Purple formazan (insoluble) | Absorbance | 570 | **Yes (DMSO / acid-IPA)** |
| **XTT** | Tetrazolium | Orange formazan (soluble) | Absorbance | 450–500 | No |
| **MTS** | Tetrazolium + PMS electron coupler | Purple formazan (soluble) | Absorbance | 490 | No |
| **WST-1** | Tetrazolium | Soluble formazan | Absorbance | 440 | No |
| **Resazurin** | Oxazine dye | Resorufin (pink, fluorescent) | Fluorescence | Ex 530–560 / Em 590 | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Fact:** WST-1 and MTS are often called "**one-step MTT**" assays because they do not require a solubilization step, making them amenable to **high-throughput screening (HTS)** in 96-well or 384-well plate formats — a key advantage in pharmaceutical cytotoxicity screening.

### 🔬 C. Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH) Release Assay

**The Why:** LDH is a stable cytoplasmic enzyme (~**140 kDa**) present in all cell types. It is released into the surrounding culture medium **only when the plasma membrane is severely damaged** (i.e., late apoptosis or necrosis). The amount of LDH in the supernatant is therefore a direct quantitative marker of **membrane-rupture cytotoxicity**.

**The How:** The LDH detection reaction is a coupled enzymatic assay:
- LDH catalyzes: **Lactate + NAD⁺ → Pyruvate + NADH**
- Then, the diaphorase / INT dye system converts NADH into a colored formazan, measured at **λ ≈ 490–500 nm**.

### 🔬 D. ATP-Luminescence Assay (the most sensitive)

**The Why:** Adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP) is the universal cellular energy currency. Dead cells rapidly deplete ATP (intracellular ATP is hydrolyzed within minutes of cell death). Thus, **ATP level is a direct, exquisitely sensitive marker of viability** — the most sensitive method commercially available.

**The How:** Luciferase enzyme catalyzes:

$$\text{ATP} + \text{D-Luciferin} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Firefly Luciferase}} \text{Oxyluciferin} + \text{AMP} + \text{PP}_i + \text{CO}_2 + \text{Light}$$

Light output (Relative Light Units, RLU) is measured in a **luminometer**. Detection limit: **~10 cells per well**.

### 🔬 E. Apoptosis-Specific Cytotoxicity Assays

For drug discovery, distinguishing **apoptosis (programmed cell death)** from **necrosis (accidental cell death)** is critical:

| Method | Detects | Principle |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| **Annexin V / PI dual staining** | Early apoptosis | Annexin V binds phosphatidylserine (PS) exposed on outer leaflet of apoptotic cell membrane; PI counterstains late apoptotic / necrotic nuclei |
| **TUNEL assay** | DNA fragmentation (late apoptosis) | Terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT) labels 3′-OH ends of fragmented DNA with labeled dUTP |
| **Caspase-3/7 activity assay** | Apoptotic executioner pathway | Fluorogenic / luminescent substrates cleaved by active caspase-3/7 |
| **Comet assay (single-cell gel electrophoresis)** | DNA strand breaks | Damaged DNA migrates further in electrophoresis, forming a "comet tail" |

## 2.3 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations — these are the most frequently tested formulas in KTU university exams for this topic.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---------------------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{Viability (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Number of viable cells}}{\text{Total number of cells (viable + non-viable)}} \times 100$ | Cell counts in cells/mL or cells/well | **Universal viability equation** |
| 2 | $\text{IC}_{50} = \text{Concentration of drug causing 50\% reduction in viability / metabolic activity}$ | Concentration in µM, µg/mL, or nM | **Half-maximal inhibitory concentration** |
| 3 | $\text{Cytotoxicity (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{OD}_{\text{sample}} - \text{OD}_{\text{low control}}}{\text{OD}_{\text{high control}} - \text{OD}_{\text{low control}}} \times 100$ | OD = Optical Density at 570 nm (MTT) | Low control = untreated; High control = Triton-X or DMSO-killed |
| 4 | $\text{Hemocytometer count} = \text{Average count per square} \times \text{Dilution factor} \times 10^4$ | Cells/mL | The $10^4$ is the chamber constant for a standard Neubauer (0.1 mm depth, 1 mm² area) |
| 5 | $\text{LDH Release (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Abs}_{\text{sample}} - \text{Abs}_{\text{background}}}{\text{Abs}_{\text{max LDH}} - \text{Abs}_{\text{background}}} \times 100$ | Absorbance at 490 nm | Max LDH = lysed positive control |
| 6 | $\text{ATP (RLU)} \propto \text{Number of viable cells}$ | Light units from luminometer | Linear over 4–5 orders of magnitude |
| 7 | $\text{Z'-factor} = 1 - \dfrac{3(\sigma_{\text{pos}} + \sigma_{\text{neg}})}{\vert \mu_{\text{pos}} - \mu_{\text{neg}} \vert}$ | σ = std. dev.; μ = mean of positive / negative controls | $Z' \geq 0.5$ indicates excellent assay quality for HTS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The **Z'-factor** (formula 7) is not just academic — it is the **FDA-recommended statistical parameter** to validate a cytotoxicity / drug-screening assay for **High-Throughput Screening (HTS)** in pharmaceutical R&D. KTU examiners often include this in 14-mark questions on HTS.

## 2.4 Engineering Significance

- **Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing:** In monoclonal antibody (mAb) production using **CHO cells**, daily viability measurements guide the decision to harvest the bioreactor (usually at viability drop below 70%) to maximize product quality and minimize host-cell-protein contamination.
- **Regenerative Medicine & Tissue Engineering:** Viability of cells in **3D scaffolds** (e.g., hydrogels, decellularized matrices) is monitored before implantation to satisfy **regulatory biocompatibility standards** (ISO 10993).
- **Cancer Drug Discovery:** Cytotoxicity IC₅₀ values of novel chemotherapeutic candidates are determined against cancer cell lines (HeLa, MCF-7, A549) versus normal cell lines to assess **selectivity index (SI)**.

$$\text{Selectivity Index (SI)} = \frac{\text{IC}_{50} \text{ on normal cells}}{\text{IC}_{50} \text{ on cancer cells}}$$

A higher SI means the drug is more toxic to cancer cells relative to normal cells, indicating therapeutic potential.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Hemocytometer-Based Trypan Blue Viability Calculation

> **Problem Statement (Modeled after KTU-style numerical):**
> A researcher harvests a CHO cell culture. Using a Neubauer hemocytometer (chamber depth = 0.1 mm, 1 mm² counting area), the following counts are obtained from the 4 large corner squares:
> - Square 1: 82 viable, 18 non-viable
> - Square 2: 78 viable, 22 non-viable
> - Square 3: 85 viable, 15 non-viable
> - Square 4: 75 viable, 25 non-viable
> The cell suspension was diluted 1:1 (cells:trypan blue) before counting.
> Calculate: (a) Total cell concentration, (b) Viable cell concentration, (c) % Viability.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Calculate the average count per square.**

$$\text{Average viable cells per square} = \frac{82 + 78 + 85 + 75}{4} = \frac{320}{4} = 80 \text{ cells}$$

$$\text{Average non-viable cells per square} = \frac{18 + 22 + 15 + 25}{4} = \frac{80}{4} = 20 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 2: Calculate average total cells per square.**

$$\text{Average total} = 80 + 20 = 100 \text{ cells per square}$$

**Step 3: Apply the hemocytometer formula.**

The chamber constant for a standard Neubauer hemocytometer is:

$$C = \frac{1}{\text{Volume of one large square}} = \frac{1}{1 \text{ mm}^2 \times 0.1 \text{ mm}} = \frac{1}{0.1 \text{ mm}^3} = \frac{1}{10^{-4} \text{ mL}} = 10^4 \text{ per mL}$$

Apply the viability equation with the dilution factor of 2 (since the cells were diluted 1:1 with trypan blue):

$$\text{Total cell concentration} = \text{Average total} \times \text{Dilution factor} \times 10^4$$

$$\text{Total cell concentration} = 100 \times 2 \times 10^4 = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ cells/mL}$$

**Step 4: Calculate viable cell concentration separately.**

$$\text{Viable cell concentration} = 80 \times 2 \times 10^4 = 1.6 \times 10^6 \text{ cells/mL}$$

**Step 5: Calculate percentage viability using Equation 1 from the formula sheet.**

$$\text{Viability (\%)} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^6}{2.0 \times 10^6} \times 100 = 80\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Students often forget to apply the dilution factor when the trypan blue stain is added. This is a guaranteed **1–2 mark loss** in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: MTT-Based Cytotoxicity & IC₅₀ Determination

> **Problem Statement:**
> An MTT assay is performed in a 96-well plate to assess the cytotoxicity of a novel anti-cancer compound "Drug X" against HeLa cells. The following absorbance values (OD at 570 nm) are obtained after 48 h treatment:

| Group | OD at 570 nm |
|-------|--------------|
| Untreated control (low control) | 0.850 |
| Positive kill control (Triton-X 0.1% lysed) | 0.120 |
| Drug X — 10 µM | 0.490 |
| Drug X — 30 µM | 0.290 |
| Drug X — 100 µM | 0.160 |

> **Calculate:** (a) % Cytotoxicity at each drug concentration, (b) Estimate the IC₅₀ using linear interpolation between the bracketing concentrations.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Apply the cytotoxicity equation (Equation 3 from formula sheet).**

For each drug concentration, the formula is:

$$\text{Cytotoxicity (\%)} = \frac{\text{OD}_{\text{sample}} - \text{OD}_{\text{low control}}}{\text{OD}_{\text{high control}} - \text{OD}_{\text{low control}}} \times 100$$

**Step 2: Subtract values for 10 µM Drug X.**

$$\text{Cytotoxicity}_{10\,\mu M} = \frac{0.490 - 0.850}{0.120 - 0.850} \times 100 = \frac{-0.360}{-0.730} \times 100 = 49.3\%$$

**Step 3: Subtract values for 30 µM Drug X.**

$$\text{Cytotoxicity}_{30\,\mu M} = \frac{0.290 - 0.850}{0.120 - 0.850} \times 100 = \frac{-0.560}{-0.730} \times 100 = 76.7\%$$

**Step 4: Subtract values for 100 µM Drug X.**

$$\text{Cytotoxicity}_{100\,\mu M} = \frac{0.160 - 0.850}{0.120 - 0.850} \times 100 = \frac{-0.690}{-0.730} \times 100 = 94.5\%$$

**Step 5: Bracket the IC₅₀ (50% inhibition).**
- 10 µM → 49.3% cytotoxicity (just below 50%)
- 30 µM → 76.7% cytotoxicity (well above 50%)

So the IC₅₀ lies between 10 µM and 30 µM. Apply linear interpolation:

$$\text{IC}_{50} = C_1 + \frac{(C_2 - C_1) \times (50 - \%_{\text{Cyt}_1})}{(\%_{\text{Cyt}_2} - \%_{\text{Cyt}_1})}$$

$$\text{IC}_{50} = 10 + \frac{(30 - 10) \times (50 - 49.3)}{(76.7 - 49.3)}$$

$$\text{IC}_{50} = 10 + \frac{20 \times 0.7}{27.4} = 10 + 0.51 = 10.51\,\mu M$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The estimated IC₅₀ is ~10.5 µM.** This is the drug concentration required to kill 50% of HeLa cells. For a more accurate IC₅₀, a non-linear 4-parameter logistic (Hill) curve fit would be used, but linear interpolation is the method expected in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Assay Should You Choose?

| Assay | Best Used For | Sensitivity | Speed | Cost | Live/Dead Distinction? | HTS-Compatible? |
|-------|---------------|-------------|-------|------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Trypan Blue | Routine subculture, manual count | Low (only dead cells) | ~5 min | Very low | Yes | No |
| **MTT** | Gold-standard viability / cytotoxicity | Medium-High | 3–4 h | Low | Indirect (metabolic) | Yes (96-well) |
| **XTT / MTS / WST-1** | Improved MTT (no solubilization) | High | 1–4 h | Medium | Indirect (metabolic) | **Yes (preferred HTS)** |
| **Resazurin** | Long-term studies, non-toxic | High | 1–4 h | Medium | Indirect (metabolic) | Yes |
| **LDH Release** | Necrosis, in vitro toxicology | Medium | ~30 min | Medium | Indirect (membrane rupture) | Yes |
| **ATP-Luciferase** | Most sensitive, low cell numbers | **Very High** | ~10 min | **High** | Indirect (ATP) | **Yes (gold HTS)** |
| **Annexin V / PI** | Distinguishes apoptosis vs. necrosis | High | 1–2 h | High | Yes (early vs. late) | Flow cytometry |
| **Clonogenic** | Long-term proliferative capacity | Low (slow) | 7–14 days | Low | Functional | No |
| **TUNEL** | Late apoptosis, DNA fragmentation | High | 4–6 h | High | Yes (DNA breaks) | Limited |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked "which assay is most suitable for high-throughput drug screening?" — the expected correct answer is **ATP-luciferase** (highest sensitivity) or **MTS / WST-1** (best cost-sensitivity balance). Examiners penalize students who recommend Trypan Blue for HTS — it is a manual, low-throughput technique.

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define cell viability. List any two methods used to measure cell viability in animal cell culture.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Cell viability is defined as the quantitative measure of the proportion of living, metabolically active, and structurally intact cells in a given cell population, typically expressed as a percentage of the total cell count.

$$\text{Viability (\%)} = \frac{\text{Number of viable cells}}{\text{Total number of cells}} \times 100$$

**Two methods:**

1. **Dye Exclusion Assay (e.g., Trypan Blue):** Viable cells with intact plasma membranes exclude the anionic dye trypan blue, whereas non-viable cells absorb it and appear blue under a phase-contrast microscope. Cells are counted using a Neubauer hemocytometer.

2. **MTT Assay:** MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide], a yellow tetrazolium salt, is reduced by mitochondrial dehydrogenase enzymes of metabolically active (live) cells into a purple-colored insoluble formazan product. The absorbance is measured spectrophotometrically at **570 nm**, and the intensity is directly proportional to the number of viable cells.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the principle of the LDH (Lactate Dehydrogenase) release assay for measuring cytotoxicity?**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH) is a stable cytoplasmic enzyme (~140 kDa) found in virtually all animal cells. In healthy cells with intact plasma membranes, LDH is retained intracellularly. However, when the cell membrane is severely damaged (as in late apoptosis or necrosis), LDH is released into the surrounding culture medium.

The amount of LDH released into the supernatant is quantified using a coupled enzymatic reaction in which LDH catalyzes the conversion of lactate to pyruvate with the reduction of NAD⁺ to NADH. The NADH then drives the reduction of a tetrazolium salt (e.g., INT) into a colored formazan product, measured at **λ ≈ 490 nm**.

$$\text{Lactate} + \text{NAD}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{LDH}} \text{Pyruvate} + \text{NADH}$$

The intensity of the colored product is directly proportional to the extent of membrane damage and thus the degree of cytotoxicity. LDH release is therefore a **marker of necrotic / late-apoptotic cytotoxicity**, as opposed to early apoptosis which is typically detected by Annexin V.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions in PEBBT411 typically feature an internal choice between two 14-mark questions, each with two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. The complete model answers are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the principle, procedure, advantages, and limitations of the **MTT assay** for measuring cell viability and cytotoxicity. Add a note on its suitability for high-throughput screening. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Principle:**
The MTT assay is a colorimetric method based on the ability of metabolically active (viable) cells to reduce the yellow tetrazolium salt MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] into a purple-colored, water-insoluble formazan product. The reduction is catalyzed by mitochondrial (and to some extent cytoplasmic) **NAD(P)H-dependent dehydrogenase enzymes**. Since only living, metabolically active cells can perform this reduction, the absorbance of the solubilized formazan is directly proportional to the number of viable cells.

**2. Procedure (Step-by-step):**
- **Step 1:** Cells (e.g., CHO, HeLa, or hybridoma) are harvested in log phase, washed in PBS, and counted.
- **Step 2:** Cells are seeded into a 96-well microplate at a density of **5 × 10³ to 1 × 10⁵ cells/well** in 100–200 µL of complete growth medium.
- **Step 3:** Cells are incubated at **37 °C in a 5% CO₂ humidified incubator** for **24 hours** to allow attachment.
- **Step 4:** Test compounds (drug candidates) are added at serial dilutions (e.g., 0.1, 1, 10, 100 µM) in triplicate wells. Untreated cells serve as negative controls; cells lysed with 0.1% Triton-X serve as positive (kill) controls.
- **Step 5:** Plates are incubated for **24 to 72 hours** depending on the experimental design.
- **Step 6:** **10–20 µL of MTT stock solution (5 mg/mL in PBS)** is added to each well, giving a final concentration of approximately **0.5 mg/mL**, and incubated for **3–4 hours** at 37 °C.
- **Step 7:** The medium is carefully aspirated, and the insoluble purple formazan crystals are **solubilized** by adding **100–150 µL of DMSO** (or acidified isopropanol, or 10% SDS in 0.01 N HCl) per well.
- **Step 8:** Absorbance is measured at **570 nm** (primary wavelength) and **630 nm** (reference, to subtract background) using a **microplate spectrophotometer (ELISA reader)**.

**3. Advantages:**
- Simple, rapid, and inexpensive.
- Quantitative and amenable to 96-well plate format.
- Widely validated, accepted by regulatory bodies (OECD, ISO).
- Does not require radioactive or sophisticated instrumentation.
- Can be used on both adherent and suspension cells.

**4. Limitations:**
- Measures **metabolic activity**, not actual cell number — may overestimate viability if cells are metabolically active but not proliferating.
- The formazan solubilization step is cumbersome and introduces variability.
- MTT itself can be mildly toxic to cells at high concentrations.
- Sensitive to culture conditions (pH, glucose, serum).
- Cannot distinguish apoptosis from necrosis.

**5. Note on HTS Suitability:**
The classical MTT assay, while widely used, is not ideal for high-throughput screening (HTS) because of the **solubilization step**. For HTS applications, the **one-step MTT analogs** — namely **MTS, XTT, and WST-1** — are preferred. These substrates produce **water-soluble formazan products** that do not require a separate solubilization step, enabling direct "add-and-read" workflows. Combined with automated liquid-handling robotics, WST-1 and ATP-luciferase assays are the gold standard for modern HTS drug-screening platforms.

---

**(b)** Explain the principle and procedure of the **ATP-luminescence assay**. Why is it considered the **most sensitive** method for cell viability measurement? Mention any **two industrial applications**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Principle:**
The ATP-luminescence assay is based on the quantitative measurement of intracellular **adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP)**, the universal energy currency of living cells. The detection reaction is catalyzed by the **firefly luciferase enzyme** in the presence of its substrate D-luciferin, molecular oxygen (O₂), and Mg²⁺ ions:

$$\text{ATP} + \text{D-Luciferin} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Firefly Luciferase, Mg}^{2+}} \text{Oxyluciferin} + \text{AMP} + \text{PP}_i + \text{CO}_2 + \text{LIGHT}$$

The intensity of emitted light (measured in Relative Light Units, RLU) is directly proportional to the ATP concentration, which in turn is proportional to the number of viable cells. Because ATP is rapidly hydrolyzed upon cell death (within minutes), the assay is exquisitely specific to living cells.

**2. Procedure:**
- **Step 1:** Cells are seeded in 96-well or 384-well white/opaque microplates (white plates are preferred to maximize luminescence signal and minimize cross-talk).
- **Step 2:** After test compound treatment (24–72 h), an **equal volume of ATP-releasing reagent** (containing a detergent such as Triton-X or a proprietary lysis buffer) is added to lyse the cells and release intracellular ATP.
- **Step 3:** The **luciferase/luciferin reagent** is added.
- **Step 4:** After a brief equilibration period (typically 1–10 minutes), the **luminescence is measured using a luminometer or microplate luminometer** with an integration time of 0.1–10 seconds per well.

**3. Why It Is the Most Sensitive Method:**
- **Ultra-low detection limit:** Capable of detecting as few as **~10 cells per well**, far more sensitive than MTT (which requires ~1,000–10,000 cells).
- **Wide linear dynamic range:** Linear over **4 to 5 orders of magnitude** of cell number, allowing accurate quantification from very low to very high cell densities without dilution.
- **Speed:** The entire assay (after cell treatment) takes only **~10 minutes**, compared to 3–4 hours for MTT.
- **No solubilization, no washing, no radioactive waste** — fully compatible with HTS and Good Laboratory Practice (GLP) standards.
- **High Z'-factor:** Typically > 0.7 in optimized assays, indicating excellent robustness for HTS.

**4. Two Industrial Applications:**

1. **High-Throughput Drug Screening in Pharmaceutical R&D:** Used to screen libraries of 100,000+ compounds for cytotoxic / cytostatic activity against cancer cell lines (HeLa, MCF-7) and to determine IC₅₀ values in dose-response studies.

2. **Bioprocess Monitoring in Mammalian Cell Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing:** Used in real-time monitoring of CHO-cell bioreactor cultures to determine the optimal harvest time (typically when viability drops below 70–80%) for maximizing monoclonal antibody (mAb) yield and product quality, as well as for **contamination detection** (microbial contamination rapidly depletes ATP in non-microbial culture).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice 2 (Alternative Set)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Set B]**

**(a)** Compare **dye exclusion assays** (e.g., Trypan Blue) and **metabolic activity assays** (e.g., MTT) for measuring cell viability. Discuss their principles, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Dye Exclusion (Trypan Blue)** | **Metabolic Activity (MTT)** |
|-----------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Principle** | Live cells with intact plasma membranes exclude the anionic trypan blue dye; dead cells absorb it and appear blue. | Live cells reduce yellow MTT to purple formazan via mitochondrial dehydrogenase enzymes. |
| **What it measures** | **Membrane integrity** (binary: live vs. dead) | **Metabolic activity / mitochondrial function** (continuous variable) |
| **Readout** | Manual counting under phase-contrast microscope (100×) | Spectrophotometric absorbance at 570 nm |
| **Time required** | ~5–10 minutes (very fast) | 3–4 hours (incubation) + solubilization |
| **Sensitivity** | Low — detects only membrane-ruptured dead cells | Medium-high — detects subtle metabolic changes |
| **Subjectivity** | Operator-dependent; inter-observer variability | Objective, instrument-based |
| **HTS compatibility** | **No** — manual, low throughput | Moderate (96-well) — but high throughput with MTS/WST-1 |
| **Cost** | Very low | Low–moderate |
| **Distinguishes apoptosis vs. necrosis** | No | No (but LDH release + Annexin V do) |
| **Major limitations** | Cannot detect early apoptosis; only dead cells; time-sensitive (over-staining) | Cannot distinguish viable-but-non-proliferating cells; solubilization step; affected by pH/glucose |

**Conclusion:**
Dye exclusion assays are best suited for **routine, rapid checks** during cell subculture and passaging. Metabolic activity assays (MTT, XTT, MTS) are more quantitative and amenable to multi-well formats, making them the **preferred choice for cytotoxicity / drug-screening studies**. For ultra-sensitive, high-throughput applications, the ATP-luciferase assay is the gold standard.

---

**(b)** What is the **IC₅₀ value**? Explain how it is determined from an MTT-based cytotoxicity assay. An MTT assay is performed on HeLa cells with a novel compound at the following concentrations and absorbance values (at 570 nm): Control (untreated) = 0.900; Triton-X killed = 0.110; 1 µM = 0.870; 5 µM = 0.720; 10 µM = 0.540; 50 µM = 0.250; 100 µM = 0.150. **Calculate the IC₅₀** using linear interpolation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition of IC₅₀:**
The **IC₅₀ (Half-Maximal Inhibitory Concentration)** is defined as the concentration of a test compound (drug) that reduces the cell viability / metabolic activity by **50%** compared to the untreated (negative) control. It is the most widely used quantitative metric for comparing the cytotoxic potency of different compounds. IC₅₀ is typically expressed in micromolar (µM) or nanomolar (nM) units.

**2. How IC₅₀ Is Determined from MTT Data:**

- **Step 1:** Cells are seeded in 96-well plates and treated with a series of compound concentrations (typically a 7–10 point log-dilution series, e.g., 0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000 µM, run in triplicate).
- **Step 2:** After 24–72 h, MTT is added; formazan is solubilized; absorbance at 570 nm is measured.
- **Step 3:** The **% viability** at each concentration is calculated as:

$$\text{\% Viability} = \frac{\text{OD}_{\text{treated}}}{\text{OD}_{\text{untreated control}}} \times 100$$

- **Step 4:** A **dose-response curve** is plotted (concentration on log-scale x-axis, % viability on y-axis).
- **Step 5:** The IC₅₀ is read from the curve as the concentration corresponding to **50% viability** (i.e., 50% cytotoxicity). For accurate estimation, a **non-linear 4-parameter logistic (Hill) regression** is fitted; for KTU exam purposes, **linear interpolation** between the two concentrations bracketing 50% is acceptable.

**3. Worked Calculation for the Given Data:**

First, compute % viability at each concentration:

| Concentration | OD (570 nm) | % Viability = (OD/0.900) × 100 | % Cytotoxicity |
|----------------|-------------|-------------------------------|----------------|
| Untreated control | 0.900 | 100% | 0% |
| Triton-X killed | 0.110 | 12.2% | 87.8% (positive control) |
| 1 µM | 0.870 | 96.7% | 3.3% |
| 5 µM | 0.720 | 80.0% | 20.0% |
| 10 µM | 0.540 | **60.0%** | 40.0% |
| 50 µM | 0.250 | **27.8%** | 72.2% |
| 100 µM | 0.150 | 16.7% | 83.3% |

The **50% viability** mark lies **between 10 µM (60% viable) and 50 µM (27.8% viable)**. Apply linear interpolation:

$$\text{IC}_{50} = C_1 + \frac{(C_2 - C_1) \times (50 - \%V_1)}{(\%V_2 - \%V_1)}$$

$$\text{IC}_{50} = 10 + \frac{(50 - 10) \times (50 - 60.0)}{(27.8 - 60.0)}$$

$$\text{IC}_{50} = 10 + \frac{40 \times (-10.0)}{(-32.2)} = 10 + 12.42 = 22.42\,\mu M$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The estimated **IC₅₀ ≈ 22.4 µM** for the novel compound on HeLa cells.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout — How Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dilution factor** in hemocytometer calculations (1:1 with trypan blue = 2× correction) → **2-mark loss**.
> 2. **Confusing viability and cytotoxicity equations** — these are mirror images. % Viability = (Sample/control) × 100; % Cytotoxicity = 100 − % Viability, or use the formula sheet's equation with low and high controls.
> 3. **Forgetting the solubilization step** in the MTT assay — a 7-mark answer that omits the DMSO step is marked incomplete.
> 4. **Incorrect wavelength** — stating 450 nm or 595 nm for MTT is wrong; the correct primary is **570 nm** with a **630 nm** reference.
> 5. **Confusing MTT with MTS / XTT / WST-1** — students sometimes mix up whether the product is soluble. The original MTT formazan is **insoluble** and requires solubilization; XTT, MTS, and WST-1 formazans are **soluble**.
> 6. **Not stating assay conditions** — answers that omit the **37 °C, 5% CO₂, humidified incubator** and exposure times lose 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Linear interpolation mistakes** in IC₅₀ calculation — students often pick the wrong pair of bracketing concentrations or invert the fraction.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision — Memorize the Following Before KTU Exams:**

- ⭐ **Cell Viability = (Viable cells / Total cells) × 100** — the universal equation.
- ⭐ **Trypan Blue (0.4% w/v)** is a **membrane-integrity** dye; staining time **2–5 minutes**; **100× phase-contrast** microscopy; **Neubauer hemocytometer chamber constant = 10⁴**.
- ⭐ **MTT → insoluble purple formazan, λ = 570 nm, requires solubilization in DMSO.**
- ⭐ **XTT, MTS, WST-1 → soluble formazan, no solubilization, "one-step MTT" for HTS.**
- ⭐ **Resazurin → Resorufin, fluorescent, λ_ex ≈ 530–560 nm, λ_em ≈ 590 nm, non-toxic to cells.**
- ⭐ **LDH release assay = marker of membrane rupture (late apoptosis / necrosis); λ = 490 nm.**
- ⭐ **ATP-luciferase = most sensitive (~10 cells), fastest (~10 min), but most expensive; HTS gold standard.**
- ⭐ **Annexin V binds phosphatidylserine (PS) on outer leaflet of apoptotic cells; PI counterstain for late apoptotic/necrotic nuclei → flow cytometry is the readout.**
- ⭐ **TUNEL assay = labels DNA strand breaks (3′-OH) with labeled dUTP via TdT enzyme.**
- ⭐ **IC₅₀ = drug concentration causing 50% loss of viability; determined from dose-response curve, using linear interpolation for KTU exams.**
- ⭐ **Cytotoxicity (%) = (OD_sample − OD_low_control) / (OD_high_control − OD_low_control) × 100.**
- ⭐ **Selectivity Index (SI) = IC₅₀(normal cells) / IC₅₀(cancer cells); SI > 2 indicates therapeutic selectivity.**
- ⭐ **Z'-factor ≥ 0.5 = excellent HTS assay quality; < 0 = unusable.**
- ⭐ **Standard culture conditions: 37 °C, 5% CO₂, ~95% relative humidity, pH 7.2–7.4, isotonic medium (often DMEM/RPMI + 5–10% FBS).**
- ⭐ **Clonogenic assay = measures long-term proliferative capacity; readout = colony formation after 7–14 days; staining with crystal violet (0.5% w/v in 25% methanol).**
- ⭐ **Healthy culture viability threshold: > 80% (industrial: > 85% during log phase).**
- ⭐ **Dye exclusion assays detect only dead cells, NOT early apoptosis — major limitation.**
- ⭐ **Metabolic assays (MTT/MTS) may overestimate viability in metabolically active but non-proliferating (senescent) cells.**
- ⭐ **For apoptosis vs. necrosis distinction → Annexin V/PI dual staining is the gold standard; LDH release detects necrosis specifically; TUNEL detects late apoptosis.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cultured Cells: Biology and Characterization - Characteristics of Cultured Cells, Measurement of Growth Parameters, Cell Adhesion, Cell Proliferation and Differentiation, Identification of Specific Cell Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT411 - PLANT AND ANIMAL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cultured Cells: Biology and Characterization - Characteristics of Cultured Cells, Measurement of Growth Parameters, Cell Adhesion, Cell Proliferation and Differentiation, Identification of Specific Cell Lines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Cultured Cells?

A **cultured cell** is a cell that has been removed from its original tissue or organ and is being maintained, propagated, and studied *in vitro* (i.e., in an artificial environment such as a culture flask, Petri dish, or bioreactor) under controlled physicochemical conditions (temperature, pH, gas exchange, and nutrient supply).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Cultured cells are eukaryotic (animal) or prokaryotic cells that have been disaggregated from the parent tissue and propagated as primary cultures or as established (continuous) cell lines on/in a suitable nutrient medium under sterile, controlled laboratory conditions.*

### 🏠 Real-World Analogy: "The Cell Hotel"

Imagine taking a fish out of a large ocean (the original tissue) and placing it into a well-maintained aquarium (the culture vessel):

| Ocean (Tissue) | Aquarium (Culture Flask) |
|---|---|
| Vast, uncontrolled environment | Closed, sterile, controlled environment |
| Cells interact with many neighbors | Cells interact only with medium + flask surface |
| Constant nutrient/waste flow | **Batch-fed** — nutrients limited, waste accumulates |
| 3D matrix (collagen, laminin, fibronectin) | 2D plastic/glass surface (or 3D hydrogel in advanced systems) |

Just as the fish needs clean water, oxygen, and food, the cultured cell needs **medium (DMEM/RPMI), 5% CO₂, 37 °C temperature, and ~95% humidity**. If you forget to feed it, the cells will eventually "pass away" (enter the **decline phase** of the growth curve).

---

## 1.2 Characteristics of Cultured Cells

Cultured cells exhibit a finite set of measurable, observable features that distinguish them from cells *in vivo*. The KTU 2024 syllabus highlights the following canonical characteristics:

### 🔬 A. Morphological Characteristics

1. **Shape** – Cells may be:
   - **Epithelial-like (E):** polygonal, flat, attach firmly in "pavement" sheets.
   - **Fibroblast-like (F):** spindle-shaped, bipolar, migratory.
   - **Lymphoblast-like:** spherical, suspension-growing, non-adherent.
   - **Mixed:** heterogeneous populations in primary cultures.

2. **Size** – Typically **10–30 μm** for most mammalian cells; measured using an **inverted phase-contrast microscope** with a calibrated ocular micrometer.

3. **Nuclear-to-Cytoplasmic (N:C) Ratio** – A diagnostic parameter. Transformed/cancer cells show **higher N:C ratios** (large nucleus relative to cytoplasm).

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Fact:** The **N:C ratio** is one of the *earliest visible markers* of cellular transformation and is routinely checked during characterization of new cell lines.

### 🧬 B. Physiological / Behavioral Characteristics

- **Adherence or Suspension:** Cells are classified as **adherent** (anchorage-dependent) or **suspension** (anchorage-independent).
- **Doubling Time (DT):** Period required for the population to double in number; commonly **18–24 hours** for many continuous cell lines.
- **Plating Efficiency (PE):** The percentage of seeded cells that successfully attach and form colonies. Healthy cultures have PE > **70%**.
- **Saturation Density:** The maximum cell number achievable per unit area (e.g., per cm²) before contact inhibition sets in.
- **Contact Inhibition:** Adherent normal cells stop dividing when they touch their neighbors. *Loss of contact inhibition* is a hallmark of transformed/cancerous cells.

### 🔐 C. Genetic Stability Characteristics

- **Karyotype:** Diploid (normal) vs. heteroploid (aneuploid, abnormal chromosome number).
- **Modal Chromosome Number:** Stable number maintained over passages.
- **Marker Chromosomes:** Distinct, identifiable chromosomes used for cell-line authentication.

---

## 1.3 Why Study Cultured Cells?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Cultured cells are the *foundational tool* of modern biotechnology — they are used in vaccine production (e.g., Vero cells for polio/rabies vaccine), monoclonal antibody manufacturing (CHO cells), drug screening, gene therapy, cancer research, and regenerative medicine.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cell Growth Curve (Population Dynamics)

When cells are seeded into a fresh culture vessel at low density, they undergo a predictable sequence of growth phases. This is called the **Growth Curve** and is the single most important graph in cell culture technology.

### 📊 The Four Canonical Phases

The growth curve is a plot of **cell number (or viable cell density, cells/mL) on the Y-axis** versus **time (hours) on the X-axis**.

#### Phase 1 — **Lag Phase (Adaptation Phase)**
- Cells do not divide, but are metabolically very active.
- They synthesize enzymes, ribosomes, and cytoskeletal proteins.
- They adhere to the substrate (if adherent) and spread out.
- Duration: **2–24 hours** depending on cell type and prior conditions.
- The cells are "recovering from the stress of subculture (trypsinization)."

#### Phase 2 — **Log Phase (Exponential Phase / Logarithmic Growth)**
- Cells divide at a **constant rate** — population doubles at regular intervals.
- This is the **most reproducible** and experimentally useful phase.
- Cells are healthiest here; ideal for subculturing, cryopreservation, and experiments.
- The plot appears as a **straight line on a semi-log plot** (log of cell number vs. time).

#### Phase 3 — **Stationary Phase (Plateau Phase)**
- Growth rate = Death rate → **net cell number remains constant**.
- Caused by: nutrient depletion, waste accumulation (lactate, ammonia), **contact inhibition**, pH drop.
- Cells may enter **G₀** (quiescent state).
- If left too long, cultures become unhealthy and should be subcultured before this phase.

#### Phase 4 — **Decline Phase (Death Phase)**
- Cell death exceeds cell division.
- Cells detach, become granular, and lyse.
- The medium turns yellow (acidic) due to accumulated metabolites.
- **Action required:** Do not let cultures reach this phase — discard and start fresh.

---

## 2.2 Measurement of Growth Parameters

A cell culture scientist must quantify growth using reproducible, validated methods. The KTU syllabus mandates knowledge of the following parameters and techniques:

### 📏 A. Direct Cell Counting Methods

#### 1. **Hemocytometer (Improved Neubauer Chamber)**
- A thick glass slide with a precisely etched grid of known volume.
- Each large square = **1 mm × 1 mm × 0.1 mm = 0.1 mm³ = 1 × 10⁻⁴ mL**.
- Standard protocol: load ~10–20 μL of cell suspension under a coverslip; count cells in the four corner squares + center square.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counting Rule:** Count cells touching the **top and left** borders of a square; **ignore** those touching the bottom and right borders (to avoid double-counting).

#### 2. **Electronic Particle Counter (Coulter Counter)**
- Cells pass through a small aperture; they displace electrolyte and change resistance.
- Each pulse = one cell. Gives a numerical count + size distribution.
- Faster than hemocytometer but cannot distinguish live from dead cells.

### 🎯 B. Indirect / Viability-Based Methods

#### 3. **Trypan Blue Exclusion Assay**
- **Viable cells** exclude the dye (intact membrane) → appear **bright/refractile**.
- **Dead cells** take up the dye → appear **blue**.
- Used to compute **% viability**.

#### 4. **MTT Assay (Colorimetric)**
- Yellow MTT → purple formazan crystals by mitochondrial dehydrogenase in **live** cells.
- Read at **570 nm** in a spectrophotometer.
- Measures **metabolic activity** (proxy for viability/proliferation).

#### 5. **Other Assays (KTU Mentions Briefly):**
   - **XTT, MTS** – improved tetrazolium salts.
   - **LDH release assay** – measures membrane damage (dead cells).
   - **BrdU / EdU incorporation** – measures DNA synthesis (proliferation).

---

## 2.3 Cell Adhesion

### 🧷 What is Cell Adhesion?

**Cell adhesion** is the process by which cultured cells attach to the culture substrate (plastic, glass, or ECM-coated surface). It is mediated by specific transmembrane receptor proteins called **integrins**.

### Key Molecules in Adhesion

| Molecule | Role | KTU Quick Recall |
|---|---|---|
| **Integrins** (αβ heterodimers) | Transmembrane receptors that bind ECM and link to cytoskeleton | "The feet of the cell" |
| **Fibronectin** | ECM glycoprotein; RGD motif binds α5β1 integrin | Coats plastic to promote adhesion |
| **Laminin** | Major component of basement membrane; binds α6β1 | Used for epithelial/neuronal cultures |
| **Collagen (Type I)** | Most abundant ECM protein | Coats surfaces for fibroblast/mesenchymal cells |
| **Vinculin / Talin / Paxillin** | Focal adhesion plaque proteins | Link integrins to actin cytoskeleton |
| **Cadherins** | Cell–cell adhesion (Ca²⁺-dependent) | E-cadherin in epithelia |

### Adhesion Process (Step-by-Step)

1. **Settling:** Cell sediments onto the surface (gravity).
2. **Attachment:** Initial, weak, nonspecific binding (electrostatic, hydrophobic).
3. **Spreading:** Cytoskeleton reorganizes; cell flattens out (15–60 min).
4. **Focal Adhesion Formation:** Integrins cluster; vinculin/talin recruit actin stress fibers.
5. **Stable Attachment:** Full cytoskeletal organization; cell cycle entry.

### Quantitative Measurement of Adhesion

The **Adhesion Assay** measures the percentage of seeded cells that remain attached after a defined incubation period (usually 30–120 minutes), following a standard wash protocol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The substrate **surface energy** and **wettability** directly affect adhesion. Tissue culture plastic is **plasma-treated or corona-discharge-treated** to render it hydrophilic (contact angle ≈ 50°), dramatically improving cell attachment versus untreated polystyrene.

---

## 2.4 Cell Proliferation and Differentiation

### 🔄 Proliferation
**Proliferation** = increase in cell number via mitosis. Quantified by:
- **Doubling time (DT)**
- **Population Doubling Level (PDL)**
- **Growth fraction (S-phase fraction by BrdU/EdU labeling)**

### 🧬 Differentiation
**Differentiation** = the process by which a less-specialized cell (stem/progenitor) becomes a specialized cell type (neuron, hepatocyte, myocyte, etc.). 

In culture, differentiation is often:
- **Constitutive** (e.g., C2C12 myoblasts → myotubes upon confluence).
- **Induced** by growth-factor withdrawal, addition of inducers (e.g., retinoic acid, dexamethasone), or 3D culture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Proliferation and Differentiation are often mutually exclusive:** Many cells must **exit the cell cycle (G₀)** before they can fully differentiate. Example: PC12 cells proliferate in growth medium, but differentiate into neuron-like cells when NGF (nerve growth factor) is added.

---

## 2.5 Identification of Specific Cell Lines

Once a culture is established, it **must be authenticated** to confirm identity, prevent cross-contamination, and ensure reproducibility.

### 🔍 Identification Techniques (KTU 2024 Mandate)

| Method | What It Identifies | Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Morphological Analysis** | Cell shape, size, growth pattern | Inverted phase-contrast microscopy |
| **Karyotyping / Chromosome Analysis** | Modal chromosome number, marker chromosomes | G-banding of metaphase spreads |
| **Isoenzyme Analysis** | Species of origin (e.g., LDH, G6PD isoenzymes) | Electrophoretic mobility patterns |
| **DNA Fingerprinting / STR Profiling** | Unique genetic identity of the line | PCR amplification of short tandem repeats |
| **Immunocytochemistry (ICC)** | Specific protein markers (e.g., cytokeratins, GFAP) | Antibody-based fluorescence or DAB staining |
| **Flow Cytometry (FACS)** | Surface & intracellular markers | Laser-based single-cell analysis |
| **PCR for Species-Specific Markers** | Confirms origin (e.g., human vs. mouse) | Amplifies species-specific gene segments |

> [!WARNING]
> **HeLa Contamination Crisis:** Studies show **>20% of cell lines** in use worldwide are contaminated with HeLa cells. STR profiling is the **gold-standard** for cell-line authentication. Never trust an unauthenticated line.

---

## 2.6 🧮 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cell Concentration (Hemocytometer)** | $C = \dfrac{N \times D}{V \times n_{sq}}$ | $N$ = cells counted, $D$ = dilution factor, $V$ = volume over one square (mL), $n_{sq}$ = number of squares counted |
| 2 | **Cell Concentration (Standard)** | $C = \dfrac{N_{total}}{5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mL}}$ | For hemocytometer: 5 large squares × $1 \times 10^{-4}$ mL each |
| 3 | **% Viability (Trypan Blue)** | $\%V = \dfrac{N_{live}}{N_{live} + N_{dead}} \times 100$ | Live cells = unstained |
| 4 | **Doubling Time (DT)** | $DT = \dfrac{t \times \ln(2)}{\ln(N_t / N_0)} = \dfrac{0.693 \times t}{\ln(N_t / N_0)}$ | $t$ = time interval, $N_0$ = initial, $N_t$ = final cell number |
| 5 | **Growth Rate (k)** | $k = \dfrac{\ln(N_t / N_0)}{t}$ | Cells per hour; reciprocal of mean generation time |
| 6 | **Plating Efficiency (PE)** | $\%PE = \dfrac{\text{Number of colonies formed}}{\text{Number of cells seeded}} \times 100$ | Healthy cultures: PE > 70% |
| 7 | **Population Doubling (PD)** | $PD = \dfrac{\log(N_t / N_0)}{\log 2}$ | Cumulative doubling number |
| 8 | **% Adhesion** | $\%A = \dfrac{N_{adhered}}{N_{seeded}} \times 100$ | Measured after wash step |
| 9 | **Saturation Density** | $SD = \dfrac{N_{max}}{A}$ | Cells per cm² |
| 10 | **Phase Fraction (e.g., S-phase)** | $\%S = \dfrac{N_{S\text{-phase cells}}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | From flow cytometry / BrdU labeling |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Vaccine Manufacturing** | Vero, MDCK cells for viral vaccine production |
| **Monoclonal Antibodies** | CHO cells producing therapeutic antibodies (Herceptin, Avastin) |
| **Drug Discovery** | High-throughput screening on HepG2, HeLa, A549 |
| **Regenerative Medicine** | MSC differentiation into osteo/chondro/neuronal lineages |
| **Cancer Research** | Studying transformed vs. normal cell behavior |
| **Toxicology** | Cytotoxicity assays (MTT, LDH) for chemical safety testing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Doubling Time (DT) Formula

The doubling time of a cell population is derived from first principles using exponential growth kinetics.

### Assumption
During exponential (log) phase, cells divide by binary fission at a **constant rate $k$** (per unit time). Therefore, the rate of increase in cell number is proportional to the current cell number:

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = k \cdot N$$

where $N$ = cell number at time $t$, and $k$ = specific growth rate constant (units: h⁻¹).

### Step 1 — Rearrange and Integrate

Separate variables:

$$\dfrac{dN}{N} = k \cdot dt$$

Integrate both sides from initial time $t_0$ (with cell number $N_0$) to final time $t$ (with cell number $N_t$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N_t} \dfrac{dN}{N} = \int_{t_0}^{t} k \, dt$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate Integrals

$$\ln(N_t) - \ln(N_0) = k \cdot (t - t_0)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_t}{N_0}\right) = k \cdot (t - t_0)$$

### Step 3 — Solve for k

$$k = \dfrac{\ln(N_t / N_0)}{(t - t_0)}$$

### Step 4 — Define Doubling Time

By definition, **doubling time (DT)** is the time required for $N_t = 2 \cdot N_0$. Substituting:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{2 N_0}{N_0}\right) = k \cdot DT$$

$$\ln(2) = k \cdot DT$$

$$DT = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{k}$$

Since $\ln(2) \approx 0.6931$:

$$\boxed{DT = \dfrac{0.693 \cdot (t - t_0)}{\ln(N_t / N_0)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Cells with **shorter doubling times** are preferred for industrial bioprocesses. For example, CHO-K1 cells (DT ≈ 18–22 h) are favored for monoclonal antibody production because they reach high densities rapidly.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Pattern)

### Problem
A researcher seeded **1 × 10⁵ cells** into a T-flask. After **48 hours**, the cell count was **8 × 10⁵ cells**. Calculate:
(a) The growth rate constant $k$.
(b) The doubling time DT.
(c) The expected cell number after **96 hours** of log-phase growth.

### Given
$N_0 = 1 \times 10^{5}$ cells  
$N_t = 8 \times 10^{5}$ cells  
$t - t_0 = 48$ h  

### Solution

#### Part (a) — Growth Rate k

$$k = \dfrac{\ln(N_t / N_0)}{t - t_0}$$

$$k = \dfrac{\ln(8 \times 10^{5} \, / \, 1 \times 10^{5})}{48}$$

$$k = \dfrac{\ln(8)}{48}$$

$$k = \dfrac{2.0794}{48}$$

$$\boxed{k = 0.0433\ \text{h}^{-1}}$$

#### Part (b) — Doubling Time DT

$$DT = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$$

$$DT = \dfrac{0.693}{0.0433}$$

$$\boxed{DT \approx 16.0\ \text{hours}}$$

#### Part (c) — Cell Number after 96 hours

Using exponential growth:

$$N_{96} = N_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$$

$$N_{96} = 1 \times 10^{5} \cdot e^{(0.0433 \times 96)}$$

$$N_{96} = 1 \times 10^{5} \cdot e^{4.1568}$$

$$N_{96} = 1 \times 10^{5} \cdot 63.94$$

$$\boxed{N_{96} \approx 6.4 \times 10^{6}\ \text{cells}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reality Check:** Real cell cultures **do not** grow exponentially forever. After ~96 hours, the culture enters stationary phase due to nutrient depletion. The above calculation assumes **continued log-phase conditions** (frequent medium replenishment).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Plating Efficiency

Plating efficiency quantifies the fraction of seeded cells that successfully attach and proliferate to form visible colonies.

### Definition

$$\%PE = \dfrac{\text{Number of colonies counted}}{\text{Number of cells initially seeded}} \times 100$$

### Step-by-Step Numerical Example

A scientist seeds **500 cells** into a 60 mm Petri dish. After 10 days, **385 colonies** are counted.

$$\%PE = \dfrac{385}{500} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%PE = 77\%}$$

### Interpretation Matrix (KTU Reference)

| % PE Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| **> 80%** | Excellent culture; healthy, robust line |
| **60–80%** | Good culture; acceptable for most experiments |
| **40–60%** | Marginal; consider sub-cloning or media optimization |
| **< 40%** | Poor; cells stressed or unhealthy — investigate |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cell Proliferation vs. Differentiation

| Feature | Proliferation | Differentiation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Increase in cell number via mitosis | Acquisition of specialized phenotype |
| **Cell Cycle Status** | Active cycling (G₁ → S → G₂ → M) | Often arrested in G₀/G₁ |
| **Molecular Markers** | Ki-67, PCNA, BrdU/EdU incorporation | Tissue-specific proteins (e.g., albumin in hepatocytes, GFAP in astrocytes) |
| **Inducers** | Growth factors (EGF, FGF, PDGF), serum | Lineage-specific factors (NGF, BMP-2, dexamethasone, retinoic acid) |
| **Morphological Change** | Usually minimal (cells divide) | Often dramatic (e.g., myoblasts → multinucleated myotubes) |
| **Measurement** | Cell counting, MTT, BrdU/EdU | ICC, qPCR for lineage markers, flow cytometry |
| **Mutual Exclusivity** | Generally inversely correlated in many cell types | Differentiation often requires proliferation arrest |
| **Engineering Relevance** | Maximize for biopharma production | Maximize for regenerative medicine |
