#### (b) [7 marks] Discuss **xenotransplantation** as an improvement application of transgenic animals. Explain the role of **GTKO pigs**.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1.5 marks):**
- **Xenotransplantation** = transplantation of **living cells, tissues, or organs** from an animal donor into a human recipient.
- The global shortage of human donor organs drives research into animal donors — **pigs** are preferred because their organ size, physiology, and breeding characteristics closely match human needs.

**Hyperacute Rejection Problem (2 marks):**
- The major barrier is **hyperacute rejection (HAR)** — occurring within minutes of transplant.
- Cause: humans naturally have **anti-Gal antibodies** that recognize the **α-1,3-galactose (α-Gal) epitope** present on the surface of all pig cells (except a few primate lineages).
- These antibodies trigger **complement activation** → instant destruction of the pig organ.

**GTKO Pigs (2.5 marks):**
- **GTKO = α-1,3-galactosyltransferase Knocked Out.**
- The gene **GGTA1** (encoding α-1,3-galactosyltransferase) is **disrupted** in the pig genome via **homologous recombination** or more recently **CRISPR/Cas9**.
- Result: pig cells **lack the α-Gal epitope** → host antibodies have no target → **hyperacute rejection is abolished**.
- **Example:** The **"GalSafe" pig** developed by **Revivicor (USA)** — approved by **FDA in December 2020** for use in human therapeutics and as a potential food source for allergic patients.

**Additional improvements (1 mark):**
- Modern "multitransgenic pigs" carry up to **10+ genetic modifications**, including:
  - Knockout of **GGTA1, CMAH, β4GalNT2** (triple-knockout to remove all major xenoantigens).
  - Transgenic expression of **human complement regulatory proteins** (hDAF = decay accelerating factor; hCD46; hCD55).
  - Transgenic expression of **human thrombomodulin** → reduces coagulation incompatibility.

> **Mark Distribution:** Intro 1.5 + HAR problem 2 + GTKO 2.5 + additional improvements 1.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls where students lose marks (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Mixing up founders vs. pups** in transgenesis efficiency formula — use **embryos microinjected**, not pups born.
> 2. **Forgetting the promoter** in the Super Mouse construct — it is **MT-I promoter**, NOT CMV. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Saying Dolly was a "transgenic sheep"** — Dolly (1996) was a **clone**, NOT transgenic. The first **cloned + transgenic** sheep was "**Polly**" (1997), carrying human Factor IX.
> 4. **Confusing AquAdvantage Salmon (transgenic) with triploid (sterile) salmon** — they are two different biological interventions. The AquAdvantage is **diploid and fertile**, but its eggs are sterilized before sale.
> 5. **Omitting the signal peptide** in a Pharming construct — without it, the recombinant protein is **trapped inside the cell** and not secreted into milk.
> 6. **Writing "transgenic = GMO" without explanation** — every transgenic animal is a GMO, but not every GMO is a transgenic animal (e.g., gene-edited organisms using CRISPR indel knockouts may be GMO without carrying foreign transgene).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision points for KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 — Improvements of Animals Using Transgenic Approach:**

- **Definition:** Transgenic animal = stable integration of foreign gene + heritable expression.
- **First transgenic animal:** **Super Mouse** (Palmiter & Brinster, 1982) — rat GH gene + MT-I promoter → 2× body size.
- **Pharming:** Use of transgenic animals as bioreactors; protein secreted in **milk, blood, eggs**.
  - **ATryn Goat** → human antithrombin III (2–3 g/L in milk).
  - **Rosie Cow** → human β-lactalbumin.
  - **Ruconest Rabbit** → human C1 esterase inhibitor.
- **Agricultural examples:**
  - **AquAdvantage Salmon** → chinook salmon GH + ocean pout AFP promoter → **FDA-approved 2015**, **first transgenic food animal**.
  - **Enviro-Pig** → carries *phytase* gene → reduces phosphorus excretion in manure.
- **Disease Models:**
  - **OncoMouse®** → *HER2/neu* oncogene → breast cancer model.
  - **Alzheimer's Mouse** → mutant human *APP* gene.
  - **Knockout Mouse (KO)** → gene function studies (e.g., p53 KO, CFTR KO).
- **Xenotransplantation:**
  - **GTKO Pig** → *GGTA1* gene knocked out → no α-Gal epitope → avoids hyperacute rejection.
  - **GalSafe Pig** (Revivicor) — FDA-approved Dec 2020.
  - **hDAF transgenic pigs** → express human decay-accelerating factor.
- **Methods of transgenesis:** pronuclear microinjection, retroviral transduction, sperm-mediated gene transfer (SMGT), SCNT/cloning, CRISPR/Cas9.
- **Standard Methods Mapping:**
  - **Mouse** → microinjection.
  - **Cattle / Pig / Sheep** → SCNT.
  - **Chicken** → retroviral.
  - **Fish (Salmon/Trout)** → cytoplasmic microinjection.
- **Key formulas:**
  - Transgenesis efficiency = founders ÷ embryos × 100.
  - Germline transmission = positive F1 ÷ total F1 × 100.
  - Mendelian F1 expectation = **50 %** heterozygous transgenic.
- **First cloned animal:** **Dolly the Sheep (1996)** — not transgenic. First cloned + transgenic sheep = **Polly (1997)**.
- **Safety metrics in KTU board answers:** germline transmission rate, transgene copy number (Southern blot), recombinant protein yield (g/L), Mendelian vs. non-Mendelian inheritance.
- **Bonus buzzword for viva:** "**Biopharming**" and "**Molecular Pharming**" are accepted synonyms of "Pharming."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT411 - PLANT AND ANIMAL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Animals as Bioreactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Animals as Bioreactors (Molecular Pharming / Biopharming)**
> The use of genetically engineered **transgenic animals** as living production systems to synthesize and secrete high-value recombinant therapeutic proteins, pharmaceuticals, or industrial enzymes. The desired gene construct is introduced into the animal's genome so that the protein product is expressed either in a specific tissue (commonly the mammary gland) or secreted into a readily harvestable body fluid such as **milk, blood, egg white, or urine**.

The KTU 2024 scheme defines this concept under the umbrella of **recombinant bioprocessing using eukaryotic whole-organism platforms**, distinguishing it sharply from microbial or plant cell-based production systems by emphasizing the animal's ability to perform complex **post-translational modifications (PTMs)** such as correct protein folding, disulfide bond formation, N-linked glycosylation, γ-carboxylation, and proteolytic processing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Living Factory Analogy**
> Imagine a cow as a sophisticated, self-replicating, self-maintaining chemical factory. Instead of building a stainless-steel bioreactor with pumps, agitators, and downstream chromatography columns, scientists "rewire" the cow's DNA so that the mammary gland now produces a human therapeutic protein (e.g., human antithrombin) **in every drop of milk it secretes**. The cow eats grass, the cells do the molecular biology, and the milk is the harvest. This is far cheaper and more biologically authentic than synthesizing the same protein in a fermenter.

> A more compact analogy: **"Take a cow. Insert one gene. Milk the medicine."**

## 1.3 Key Quantitative & Biological Metrics

The performance of an animal bioreactor is benchmarked using the following standard metrics (all values are **ballpark reference yields** used in industry literature):

| Metric | Standard Reference Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Expression Level (g/L)** | **0.1 – 10 g/L** | Concentration of recombinant protein in target fluid |
| **Annual Yield per Animal** | **1 – 80 kg/year** | Total recombinant protein obtained from one animal |
| **Transgenic Efficiency** | **1 – 30 %** | % of microinjected embryos that integrate the transgene |
| **Time to First Harvest** | **12 – 30 months** | From embryo transfer to first lactation/egg |
| **Lactation Cycle** | **305 days** | Standard dairy cow lactation period |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the mammary gland dominates:** Milk is harvested non-invasively, in gram-to-kilogram quantities, with well-established downstream processing infrastructure (cheese/dairy industry). The protein content of milk is naturally low (~3.5 %), so purification of the recombinant product from a small subset of milk proteins is technologically tractable.

## 1.4 Why Animals, Not Microbes or Plants?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight: Comparison Anchor**
> Microbial systems (*E. coli*, yeast) are cheap and fast but **cannot** perform complex mammalian PTMs, particularly human-type sialylation of N-glycans. Plant systems are scalable but produce **non-mammalian glycosylation patterns** (β-1,2 xylose, α-1,3 fucose) that are immunogenic in humans. **Animal cells are the only practical system for producing proteins requiring authentic human PTMs at industrial scale.**

---

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strategic Design of an Animal Bioreactor — A Six-Pillar Logic

Building a functional animal bioreactor requires solving six sequential engineering problems:

1. **Selection of Target Tissue (The "Cell Factory")**  
   The transgene must be driven by a **tissue-specific promoter** so it expresses only in the chosen organ. The common target tissues and their respective promoters are:
   - **Mammary gland** → β-lactoglobulin (BLG), β-casein, whey acidic protein (WAP) promoters
   - **Blood/serum** → albumin, transferrin, or α-globin promoters
   - **Egg white (in hens)** → ovalbumin, lysozyme promoters
   - **Urine (in mice)** → uroplakin promoters (UPIa, UPIb)
   - **Seminal plasma (in boars)** → seminal vesicle protein promoters

2. **Construction of the Transgene Cassette**  
   The expression vector contains, in 5'→3' order:  
   **Tissue-specific promoter → Signal peptide (e.g., signal peptide of the whey protein) → Therapeutic gene → Polyadenylation signal (e.g., SV40 or bovine growth hormone polyA).**  
   The signal peptide is critical because it directs the nascent protein into the secretory pathway, ultimately appearing in milk/egg/urine.

3. **Introduction into the Germ Line**  
   - **Pronuclear Microinjection (PNI)** — classical method; injects DNA into the male pronucleus of a fertilized egg.
   - **Somatic Cell Nuclear Transfer (SCNT)** — nuclear transfer into enucleated oocytes; Dolly the sheep was made this way.
   - **Lentiviral Transgenesis** — high efficiency (~80 %); used successfully in pigs and cattle.
   - **CRISPR/Cas9-mediated Knock-in (HDR)** — modern, precise integration at a "safe harbor" locus such as *Rosa26* or *CSN2* (β-casein).

4. **Embryo Transfer and Founder Generation (F0)**  
   Microinjected embryos are implanted into surrogate mothers. A fraction of the offspring (founders) carry the transgene stably integrated.

5. **Screening and Characterization**  
   - **PCR / Southern blot** of tail DNA → confirm integration
   - **RT-qPCR / Western blot / ELISA** of milk/blood → confirm expression
   - **Mass spectrometry of N-glycans** → confirm correct PTMs

6. **Breeding, Scale-up, and Bioprocessing**  
   The F0 founder is bred to create **heterozygous and then homozygous** lines, which exhibit higher expression per animal. A transgenic herd/flock is then maintained as the production platform.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Math for Bioreactor Yields**

| Equation / Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Annual Protein Yield per Animal** | $Y_{animal} = C_{milk} \times V_{lactation}$ | **kg / animal / year** | Total harvest estimate |
| **Cost per Gram of Product** | $C_{gram} = \dfrac{C_{animal-life} + C_{purification}}{Y_{animal} \times N_{animals}}$ | **USD / g** | Economic feasibility |
| **Transgenic Frequency (Mendelian)** | $f = \dfrac{n_{transgenic}}{n_{total\ offspring}}$ | **dimensionless (0 to 1)** | Germline transmission rate |
| **Homozygous vs Heterozygous Expression** | $E_{Homo} \approx 2 \times E_{Hetero}$ | **mg / mL** | Genetic scaling rule |
| **Glycosylation Efficiency** | $\eta_{glyc} = \dfrac{N_{correct-glycoforms}}{N_{total-glycoforms}} \times 100\%$ | **%** | PTM quality index |
| **Generation Interval (Cattle)** | $t_{gen} = 24\text{ – }30$ months | **months** | Scale-up time |

**Where:**
- $C_{milk}$ = recombinant protein concentration in milk (g/L)
- $V_{lactation}$ = total milk volume over one lactation cycle (L/year)
- $N_{animals}$ = number of animals in the production herd
- $N_{correct-glycoforms}$ = species with terminal sialic acid (Neu5Ac) residues
- $E_{Homo}, E_{Hetero}$ = expression in homozygous and heterozygous animals

## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility

| Sector | Application |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Recombinant antibodies, blood factors, hormones, vaccines |
| **Replacement therapy** | AAT (alpha-1 antitrypsin) for emphysema; ATryn (antithrombin) for heparin resistance |
| **Cardiovascular** | Tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) in goat milk |
| **Hemophilia** | Factor VIII / Factor IX from transgenic pig or rabbit milk |
| **Monoclonal antibodies** | Fully human mAbs expressed in goat/rabbit milk |
| **Transgenic organs (xenotransplantation)** | Pigs engineered for human-compatible heart valves (decellularized) |

## 2.4 Comparative Matrix of Production Platforms

| Property | *E. coli* | CHO Cells | Transgenic Plant | Transgenic Animal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | Low | Medium | Very Low | High (front-loaded) |
| Scale-up | Easy | Moderate | Easy (agriculture) | Slow (breeding) |
| PTM fidelity | None | Excellent | Plant-type (immunogenic) | **Human-type (excellent)** |
| Time to product | Weeks | Months | Months–Years | 18–36 months |
| Harvest | Intracellular | Supernatant | Leaves/Seeds | Milk/Blood/Egg |
| Regulatory complexity | Low | Medium | High | **Very High** |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Quantitative Derivation 1 — Annual Yield from a Transgenic Goat

> A transgenic goat herd is engineered to express human antithrombin (AT) in milk at an average concentration of **$C_{milk} = 4$ g/L**. Each goat lactates for **$L = 305$ days/year** with an average daily milk yield of **$V_{d} = 3$ L/day**. Estimate the annual recombinant AT yield per goat and the herd-level yield for a herd of **$N = 50$** goats.

### Step 1 — Total milk volume per goat per year
$$
V_{annual} = V_{d} \times L
$$
$$
V_{annual} = 3 \;\text{L/day} \times 305 \;\text{days} = 915 \;\text{L/year}
$$

### Step 2 — Annual recombinant protein per goat
$$
Y_{goat} = C_{milk} \times V_{annual}
$$
$$
Y_{goat} = 4 \;\text{g/L} \times 915 \;\text{L} = 3{,}660 \;\text{g/goat/year}
$$

### Step 3 — Herd-level annual yield
$$
Y_{herd} = N \times Y_{goat}
$$
$$
Y_{herd} = 50 \times 3{,}660 = 183{,}000 \;\text{g/year} = 183 \;\text{kg/year}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result Interpretation:** A modest herd of 50 goats produces **183 kg/year** of clinical-grade antithrombin. This single product is worth tens of millions of USD annually. The dominant cost driver is no longer the upstream production — it is **downstream purification**, GMP facility maintenance, and regulatory compliance (FDA/EMA).

---

## 3.2 Quantitative Derivation 2 — Per-Gram Production Cost

> Suppose each transgenic goat has an annualized upkeep cost (feed, housing, veterinary, labour, depreciation) of **$C_{u} = 2{,}000$ USD/year**, and downstream purification costs an additional **$C_{p} = 100$ USD per gram of purified product**. Compute the unit cost $C_{gram}$.

### Step 1 — Cost of production per animal per year
$$
C_{animal-life} = C_{u} + C_{p} \times Y_{goat}
$$
$$
C_{animal-life} = 2{,}000 + 100 \times 3{,}660 = 2{,}000 + 366{,}000 = 368{,}000 \;\text{USD}
$$

### Step 2 — Cost per gram
$$
C_{gram} = \dfrac{C_{animal-life}}{Y_{goat}}
$$
$$
C_{gram} = \dfrac{368{,}000}{3{,}660} = 100.55 \;\text{USD/g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic Insight:** Once the herd is established, the marginal cost per gram is dominated by purification (≈ **$100 USD/g**). The upstream biological production is essentially "free" sunlight + grass. This is the central economic appeal of animal bioreactors relative to stainless-steel fermenters, which can run **$1{,}000$ – $10{,}000$ USD/g** in upstream costs alone.

---

## 3.3 Quantitative Derivation 3 — Glycosylation Quality Index

> A purified batch of human tPA from transgenic goat milk is analyzed by N-glycan profiling. Out of **$N_{total} = 1{,}200$** detected glycoform peaks, **$N_{correct} = 1{,}020$** carry the human-compatible **Neu5Ac (sialic acid)** terminal cap. Compute the glycosylation quality index $\eta_{glyc}$.

### Step 1 — Direct application of the formula
$$
\eta_{glyc} = \dfrac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{glyc} = \dfrac{1{,}020}{1{,}200} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{glyc} = 0.85 \times 100\% = 85\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory Threshold:** The FDA and EMA typically require **$\eta_{glyc} \geq 80\%$** for human-compatible glycosylation in biosimilar approval. The transgenic goat batch passes this criterion, but the **15% non-human glycoforms** must be characterized and shown to be non-immunogenic during clinical trials.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Four Major Animal Bioreactor Systems

| Property | **Mammary Gland (Milk)** | **Blood/Serum** | **Egg White** | **Urine** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Animal** | Goat, Cow, Sheep, Rabbit | Pig, Cow | Chicken (transgenic hen) | Mouse |
| **Yield volume** | High (hundreds of L/year) | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| **Harvest invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Invasive (phlebotomy) | Non-invasive | Non-invasive |
| **PTM quality** | Excellent (mammary) | Excellent (hepatic) | Excellent (oviduct) | Moderate |
| **Major product** | AT, tPA, mAbs, AAT, FVIII | Hb, albumin, FVIII | mAbs, IFN-β, lysozyme peptides | Growth hormone, peptides |
| **Regulatory status** | FDA-approved products exist | Under development | Early-stage | Research only |
| **Key advantage** | Scale + purification ease | Native serum environment | Fast generation time | Easy mouse transgenesis |
| **Key limitation** | Long generation interval | Hemolysis / contamination | Glycan differences vs human | Dilute; small scale |

---

## 3.5 Classical Procedure: Pronuclear Microinjection (PNI)

> This is the historical workhorse technique for generating transgenic livestock and is **frequently asked in KTU exams**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Fertilized Egg} \xrightarrow{\text{Hormonal superovulation}} \text{Donor Female}}
$$

1. **Superovulation** — donor females (e.g., rabbits) are injected with PMSG/hCG to yield 20–30 eggs per cycle.
2. **Pronuclear isolation** — fertilized eggs are flushed, and the male pronucleus is visualized under a differential interference contrast (DIC) microscope.
3. **DNA microinjection** — a few picolitres of linearized transgene construct (typically 1,000–5,000 copies) are injected using a glass micropipette.
4. **Embryo culture** — injected zygotes are cultured briefly to the 1–2 cell stage.
5. **Surgical embryo transfer** — embryos are implanted into the oviduct of a synchronized surrogate mother.
6. **Founder screening** — offspring are genotyped by PCR/Southern blot; only the **germline-positive** founders are bred forward.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **pronuclear microinjection (random integration)** with **CRISPR knock-in (targeted integration)**. Pronuclear microinjection produces **random insertion** at unpredictable chromosomal loci, leading to position-effect variegation and inconsistent expression. This is why **founder lines must be screened** for expression level.

---

## 3.6 The SCNT (Somatic Cell Nuclear Transfer) Pathway

Unlike PNI, SCNT enables **pre-screening** of cells before generating an animal:

$$
\text{Donor cell} \xrightarrow{\text{Transfection (transgene integration)}} \text{Selected cell clone} \xrightarrow{\text{Nuclear transfer to enucleated oocyte}} \text{SCNT embryo} \xrightarrow{\text{Activation}} \text{Cloned transgenic offspring}
$$

> The first transgenic animals made by SCNT were the famous **"GFP pigs"** and the **ATryn goats** producing human antithrombin, the **first FDA-approved product from a transgenic animal** (2009).

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "animals as bioreactors" and list any two advantages of using transgenic animals for pharmaceutical protein production.**

**Model Answer:**
Animals as bioreactors (also called molecular pharming) refer to the use of **genetically engineered transgenic animals** as living production systems to express and secrete recombinant therapeutic proteins into harvestable body fluids such as milk, blood, or egg white.

**Two advantages:**
1. They perform **complex post-translational modifications** (e.g., human-type glycosylation, γ-carboxylation) that microbial systems cannot replicate.
2. The mammary gland system provides **high-volume, non-invasive harvest** with established dairy-scale downstream processing.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is the mammary gland preferred over blood as a tissue for recombinant protein expression in transgenic livestock?**

**Model Answer:**
The mammary gland is preferred because:
1. **High volumetric output:** A single cow produces thousands of liters of milk per lactation, allowing kg-scale recombinant protein yield.
2. **Non-invasive harvest:** Milk can be collected repeatedly without harming the animal, unlike blood (which requires phlebotomy and risks hemolysis/contamination).
3. **Simplified downstream processing:** Recombinant protein in milk constitutes a small fraction of total milk protein, enabling efficient chromatographic purification.
4. **Low risk of biological contamination of plasma-derived products** (e.g., no risk of endogenous viral load reaching the therapeutic product).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either OR both options** as per the KTU 2024 internal-choice scheme. Below are two complete alternative 14-mark questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEBBT411]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of animals as bioreactors. Discuss the key criteria for selecting a suitable tissue for recombinant protein production in transgenic animals.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**  
Animals as bioreactors (molecular pharming) are transgenic animals genetically engineered to express recombinant therapeutic proteins, where the animal itself functions as the upstream production unit. The protein is harvested from a specific body fluid.

**Key Criteria for Tissue Selection:**

1. **Availability of a strong, tissue-specific promoter:**  
   The promoter must drive high expression only in the chosen tissue (e.g., β-casein promoter for mammary, ovalbumin for oviduct).
2. **Capacity for post-translational modification:**  
   The tissue must be able to perform the required PTMs — N-glycosylation, disulfide bond formation, γ-carboxylation, etc.
3. **Ease of harvest:**  
   The recombinant product should accumulate in an easily collectable fluid (milk, blood, egg) without invasive procedures.
4. **Low endogenous competing proteins:**  
   Fewer background proteins in the target fluid → easier downstream purification.
5. **Stability and yield:**  
   The product must be stable in the target fluid (milk contains proteases; blood has many interfering proteins).
6. **Regulatory tractability:**  
   A clear purification tag and established safety profile is needed.

> **Example:** Goat mammary gland is preferred for human antithrombin production because β-casein promoter yields **2–5 g/L** of functional AT in milk, with correct human-type glycosylation.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe in detail the process of generating a transgenic animal by **pronuclear microinjection**. Add a note on its limitations.

**Model Answer:**

**Steps of Pronuclear Microinjection (PNI):**

1. **Superovulation of donor female** using PMSG/hCG to obtain 20–30 fertilized eggs.
2. **Flushing and isolation** of zygotes from the oviduct.
3. **Visualization of the male pronucleus** under a differential interference contrast (DIC) microscope.
4. **Microinjection** of ~1,000–5,000 copies of the linearized transgene construct into the male pronucleus using a glass micropipette.
5. **Brief in-vitro culture** of injected zygotes to confirm viability.
6. **Surgical transfer** of viable embryos into the oviduct of a synchronized surrogate mother.
7. **Gestation and birth** of F0 offspring.
8. **Genotyping** by PCR / Southern blot of tail DNA to identify founders.
9. **Expression analysis** of the target fluid (milk/blood) by ELISA / Western blot.
10. **Breeding** of confirmed founders to expand the transgenic line.

**Limitations of PNI:**

1. **Low efficiency:** Only 1–5 % of injected embryos become transgenic founders.
2. **Random integration:** Transgene inserts at random chromosomal loci, causing **position-effect variegation** (variable expression).
3. **Founder mosaicism:** Integration often occurs after the first DNA replication, producing mosaic founders requiring outbreeding.
4. **No control over copy number:** Multicopy arrays can cause gene silencing.
5. **Time and cost-intensive:** Requires surgical expertise, surrogate females, and long gestation intervals.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When asked to "describe pronuclear microinjection," students often **forget the final screening steps (PCR/Southern blot and expression analysis)**. A complete answer must include **all 10 steps** including post-natal screening. Skipping steps 8–10 will cost you 2–3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare the mammary gland, blood, and egg white as production platforms for recombinant proteins in transgenic animals. Mention at least one commercial product for each system.

**Model Answer:**

| Platform | Animal | Promoter | Key Product | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mammary Gland (Milk)** | Goat / Cow / Sheep | β-casein, WAP, BLG | **ATryn (antithrombin)** in goat milk (GTC Biotherapeutics, 2009 FDA approved) | High yield, non-invasive harvest, established dairy processing | Long generation interval (~24 months) |
| **Blood Serum** | Pig / Cow | Albumin, transferrin | **Human hemoglobin** (Hb) prototypes in pig blood | Native serum environment for plasma proteins | Invasive harvest, contamination risk |
| **Egg White** | Chicken (transgenic hen) | Ovalbumin, lysozyme | **Interferon-β / mAb candidates** in hen eggs (e.g., **Kanuma/sebelipase alfa** in eggs — Alexion) | Fast generation (~6 months to first egg), high volume per hen | Glycan differences (avian vs human) require engineering |

**Discussion:**
- **Mammary gland** is the gold standard for **high-yield, complex glycoproteins** such as AT, AAT, tPA, FVIII, mAbs.
- **Blood/serum** is best for **intravascular proteins** (e.g., albumin, hemoglobin) where secretion into plasma is natural.
- **Egg white** is ideal for **high-volume, low-cost production of mAbs** in transgenic hens; the chick matures in months, and an egg is essentially a "pre-packaged bioreactor."

> **Comparative Insight:** Although the mammary gland is the most mature platform, **egg white bioreactors are gaining traction** because of their **short generation time** and lower capital investment.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Discuss the major **applications** of animals as bioreactors in the pharmaceutical industry. Highlight any three commercial successes.

**Model Answer:**

**Major Applications:**

1. **Production of recombinant therapeutic proteins** — e.g., human antithrombin, α-1 antitrypsin, tissue plasminogen activator (tPA), clotting factors VIII and IX.
2. **Production of monoclonal antibodies (mAbs)** — fully human mAbs in goat, rabbit, or chicken egg white for cancer and autoimmune therapy.
3. **Production of vaccines** — subunit vaccines expressed in milk or eggs (low-cost oral vaccine candidates).
4. **Plasma protein replacement** — human serum albumin, hemoglobin, and immunoglobulin from transgenic livestock.
5. **Xenotransplantation source** — pigs engineered for human-compatible organ/cell transplantation (e.g., decellularized heart valves).
6. **Toxicity testing / disease models** — transgenic animals as living models of human genetic disease for drug screening (a related but distinct application).

**Three Commercial Successes:**

1. **ATryn® (antithrombin α)** — Produced in the milk of transgenic goats by GTC Biotherapeutics; **first FDA-approved product from a transgenic animal (2009)**. Used in patients with hereditary antithrombin deficiency undergoing surgery or childbirth.
2. **Ruconest® (conestat alfa)** — Recombinant C1 esterase inhibitor produced in the milk of transgenic **New Zealand White rabbits** by Pharming Group N.V.; approved in Europe (2010) and the US (2014) for hereditary angioedema.
3. **Kanuma® (sebelipase alfa)** — Recombinant human lysosomal acid lipase produced in **transgenic chicken egg white** by Alexion Pharmaceuticals; approved by FDA (2015) for LAL deficiency (Wolman disease).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Product Names:** Students often confuse **ATryn (antithrombin from goat milk)** with **Ruconest (C1-INH from rabbit milk)**. Remember the animal–product pair correctly:  
> - **Goat → Antithrombin (ATryn)**  
> - **Rabbit → C1 esterase inhibitor (Ruconest)**  
> - **Chicken → Lysosomal acid lipase (Kanuma)**  
> Confusing the source animal will cost 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Definition:** Animals as bioreactors = transgenic animals expressing recombinant therapeutic proteins in harvestable body fluids (milk, blood, egg white, urine). Also called **molecular pharming** or **biopharming**.
- ☐ **Six pillars:** (1) Tissue selection, (2) Vector design, (3) Transgene introduction, (4) Embryo transfer, (5) Screening, (6) Breeding & scale-up.
- ☐ **Key tissue-specific promoters:** β-casein (mammary), WAP (mammary), ovalbumin (egg), albumin (blood), uroplakin (urine).
- ☐ **Three methods of transgenesis:** PNI (random, low efficiency), SCNT (pre-screenable, used for ATryn), Lentiviral/CRISPR (high efficiency, modern).
- ☐ **Why mammary gland is dominant:** non-invasive, high-volume, easily purified, correct human PTMs.
- ☐ **Commercial products to memorize:**
  - **ATryn** → Goat milk → Antithrombin (FDA 2009)
  - **Ruconest** → Rabbit milk → C1-INH (EMA 2010, FDA 2014)
  - **Kanuma** → Chicken egg white → LAL/sebelipase alfa (FDA 2015)
- ☐ **PNI limitations:** Random integration, position-effect variegation, mosaicism, low efficiency (1–5 %), no copy-number control.
- ☐ **Yields to memorize:** 0.1–10 g/L in milk, 1–80 kg/year per animal, lactation ≈ 305 days, goat generation interval ≈ 24 months.
- ☐ **Glycosylation QA:** $\eta_{glyc} \geq 80\%$ is the FDA-acceptable human-compatibility threshold.
- ☐ **Animal vs Microbe vs Plant:** Animals are best for **complex human glycoproteins**; microbes for simple peptides; plants for scalable but non-human-glycosylated products.
- ☐ **Ethical & regulatory considerations:** Animal welfare, prion/TSE risk, biosafety, FDA/EMA oversight, public acceptance of "food vs pharma" use of livestock.
